questions.specification:
A student investigates the melting behaviour of three substances: X, Y, and Z. The data collected are shown below.

**Table 1. Mass of substance and temperature change during heating**

| Substance | Mass / $\text{g}$ | Time to melt / $\text{s}$ | Initial temp / $^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Final temp / $^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Melting point Observed / $^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| X | $5.00$ | $60$ | $22$ | $140$ | $134$ |
| Y | $5.00$ | $25$ | $21$ | $130$ | $106\text{--}120$ |
| Z | $5.00$ | $45$ | $23$ | $135$ | $118$ |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.1 Introduction to the particulate nature of matter > S1.1.1 Elements, Compounds and Mixtures
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.1.1 Elements, Compounds and Mixtures
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b), (d), and (e) focus on using melting point behavior (sharp vs range, reproducibility on reheating) to identify whether a substance is pure or a mixture, which is covered under Elements, Compounds and Mixtures.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.9 Intermolecular forces and physical properties
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.9 Intermolecular forces and physical properties
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question discusses melting behavior and temperature changes during heating; linking phase-change behavior/temperature profiles to physical properties can fit under how intermolecular forces relate to physical properties, though the main emphasis is purity vs mixture.

parts.content: State one piece of evidence from the data that suggests substance Y is a mixture. [1m]
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that substance Y has a melting range ($106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$) rather than a fixed melting point <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the objective
steps.content: To determine if a substance is a mixture from experimental data, we look at its physical properties. In this case, we need to examine the "Melting point Observed" column in Table 1 to see how the substances behave as they change state.

steps.title: Compare melting point behaviors
steps.content:
In chemistry, pure substances have a single, "sharp" melting point. Looking at the data:

*   **Substance X**: Melts at a fixed point of $134\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.
*   **Substance Z**: Melts at a fixed point of $118\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.
*   **Substance Y**: Melts over a temperature interval of $106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$.

steps.title: Identify the melting range
steps.content:
As covered in **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**, mixtures do not have a sharp melting point. Instead, they melt over a range of temperatures because the different components within the mixture have different individual melting points.

Since substance Y melts over the range $106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$, this is clear evidence that it is a mixture.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to state the observation regarding the temperature range.

**Evidence:** Substance Y melts over a range of temperatures ($106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$) rather than at a fixed melting point.

parts.content: Determine the average rate of temperature increase for substance Z in $^{\circ}\text{C}\ \text{s}^{-1}$. [2m]
parts.markscheme:
- **Calculate** the total change in temperature: $135\ ^{\circ}\text{C} - 23\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 112\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Determine** the rate of increase using the time from the table: $\text{Rate} = \frac{112\ ^{\circ}\text{C}}{45\ \text{s}} = 2.49\ ^{\circ}\text{C}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept $2.5\ ^{\circ}\text{C}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ if rounded to one decimal place.</note>

steps.title: Identify data for substance Z
steps.content:
To find the average rate of temperature increase, we first need to identify the specific values for substance Z from Table 1:

*   **Initial temperature**: $23\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
*   **Final temperature**: $135\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$
*   **Time taken**: $45\ \text{s}$

steps.title: Calculate total temperature change
steps.content:
Subtract the initial temperature from the final temperature to find the total change ($ \Delta T $):

$$\Delta T = 135\ ^{\circ}\text{C} - 23\ ^{\circ}\text{C} = 112\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Finding this value correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Calculate rate of increase
steps.content:
The average rate is the total change divided by the total time. 

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{\text{Total Temperature Change}}{\text{Total Time}}$$

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{112\ ^{\circ}\text{C}}{45\ \text{s}} \approx 2.49\ ^{\circ}\text{C}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

Providing the final rate with units earns the second mark (**A1**). Note that $2.5\ ^{\circ}\text{C}\ \text{s}^{-1}$ is also acceptable if rounded to one decimal place.

ti84.instructions: Enter 112 divided by 45 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 112 / 45 in a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the average rate by dividing the temperature change by time.

parts.content: Using the data, discuss the melting behaviour of substance X and substance Y. [2m]
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** that substance X melts at a sharp/fixed temperature ($134\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$), which indicates it is a pure substance <marks>A1</marks>
- **Identify** that substance Y melts over a range ($106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$), which indicates it is a mixture <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze Substance X
steps.content:
To determine the purity of a substance in chemistry, we look at its melting behavior. For Substance X, the table shows an observed melting point of:

$$134\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Because this is a single, sharp temperature, it indicates that Substance X is a **pure substance**. Pure substances have a fixed melting point because all particles in the sample require the same amount of energy to overcome intermolecular forces.

steps.title: Analyze Substance Y
steps.content:
Now let's look at Substance Y. The table provides a range for its observed melting point:

$$106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Melting over a wide temperature range is a classic sign of a **mixture**. In a mixture, the different components disrupt the regular lattice structure, causing the substance to start melting at a lower temperature and continue melting until all components have changed state.

steps.title: Summary of findings
steps.content:
In summary, based on the **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures** of your syllabus:

*   **Substance X** is a pure substance because it has a sharp melting point of $$134\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$.
*   **Substance Y** is a mixture because it melts over a range of $$106\text{--}120\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$.

Each of these observations earns 1 mark in the markscheme.

parts.content:
The substances are cooled and observed again. Only substance X reforms a solid with the same sharp melting point on reheating.

Suggest what this indicates about the type of matter in substance X. [1m]
parts.markscheme:
- **Suggest** that substance X is a pure substance with consistent, reproducible properties <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the experimental evidence
steps.content: In chemistry, we distinguish between different types of matter based on their physical properties. The question highlights two key features for substance X: it has a **sharp melting point** (a single temperature of $134^{\circ}\text{C}$) and this property is **reproducible** (it stays the same upon reheating).

steps.title: Define melting behavior
steps.content:
According to **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**, pure substances and mixtures behave differently when heated:

*   **Pure Substances:** Have fixed, characteristic physical properties. They melt at one specific, "sharp" temperature because all particles are identical and require the same amount of energy to overcome intermolecular forces.
*   **Mixtures:** Usually melt over a range of temperatures (like substance Y) because they contain different components with different melting points.

steps.title: Identify substance X
steps.content: Since substance X consistently melts at the exact same sharp temperature, we can conclude that it is a **pure substance**. Its properties are consistent and not influenced by the presence of other components that would cause a temperature range or change the melting point upon cooling and reheating.

parts.content: Suggest one improvement to this experiment that would help determine whether a substance is pure more accurately. [1m]
parts.markscheme:
- **Suggest** an improvement such as using a digital thermometer or a melting point apparatus for more precise observations <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>Accept other valid improvements: heating the sample more slowly, repeating trials, or using a smaller mass of the substance to ensure uniform heating.</note>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To determine whether a substance is pure, we look for a **sharp melting point** (a single temperature). Impure substances, like Substance Y in the table, typically melt over a wide range. To distinguish between these more accurately, we need to improve the precision of our temperature measurements.

highlights.text: determine whether a substance is pure more accurately

steps.title: Upgrade temperature resolution
steps.content: The current data shows whole numbers for most temperatures (e.g., $134^{\circ}\text{C}$), which suggests the thermometer used has low resolution. Using a **digital thermometer** with a resolution of $\pm 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ would allow us to see if the melting occurs at a specific point or over a very narrow range that might be missed with a standard glass thermometer.

steps.title: Use specialized apparatus
steps.content: Alternatively, using a dedicated **melting point apparatus** is a significant improvement. These devices often include a magnifying lens to observe the sample closely and a heating block that ensures much more uniform and controlled heating than a beaker of oil or water. This helps in identifying the exact moment the first crystal melts and the last crystal disappears.

steps.title: State the final improvement
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, providing either of these instrumental improvements will earn the mark. 

**Suggested Improvement:** Use a digital thermometer or a dedicated melting point apparatus for more precise temperature observations.

highlights.text: using a digital thermometer or a melting point apparatus for more precise observations

steps.title: Link purity to melting behavior
steps.content:
In chemistry, a pure substance typically has a **sharp melting point**, meaning it transitions from solid to liquid at one specific temperature. If a substance is impure, it usually melts over a temperature range and at a lower temperature than the pure form. 

Looking at the data for **Substance Y**, we see an observed melting range of $106\text{--}120^{\circ}\text{C}$. This wide range makes it difficult to determine if the substance is truly impure or if the experimental technique was simply too rushed.

steps.title: Evaluate current heating rates
steps.content:
To see why the current experiment might be inaccurate, let's calculate the heating rate for Substance Y. The temperature rose from $21^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $130^{\circ}\text{C}$ in only $25\text{ s}$.

$$\text{Heating Rate} = \frac{\text{Final Temp} - \text{Initial Temp}}{\text{Time}}$$

$$\text{Heating Rate} = \frac{130^{\circ}\text{C} - 21^{\circ}\text{C}}{25\text{ s}} \approx 4.36^{\circ}\text{C/s}$$ 

This is incredibly fast! In a standard laboratory melting point determination, we usually aim for a rate of about $1\text{--}2^{\circ}\text{C}$ **per minute** as we approach the melting point to ensure the thermometer and the sample are in thermal equilibrium.

calculator.description: Calculate the heating rate for Substance Y.

steps.title: Suggest controlling heating rate
steps.content:
The best improvement for accuracy is to **slow down the rate of heating** as the substance approaches its expected melting point. 

By reducing the heat intensity (e.g., turning down the hot plate or using a lower setting on the melting point apparatus), you ensure that the heat is distributed evenly throughout the sample. This prevents the outside of the sample from melting while the inside remains solid, which would otherwise result in a falsely broad melting range.

steps.title: Final improvement suggestion
steps.content:
Based on the requirement for 1 mark, a valid improvement is:

**Heat the sample more slowly, especially near the expected melting point, to ensure thermal equilibrium and a sharper temperature reading.**

steps.title: Analyze the current setup
steps.content: In the data provided, we see that the student used a mass of $5.00\text{ g}$ for each substance. In chemistry, when we want to determine the **purity** of a substance, we look for a sharp melting point. If the sample is too large, the heat may not be distributed evenly, leading to inaccurate results.

steps.title: Identify thermal uniformity issues
steps.content: When heating a relatively large mass like $5.00\text{ g}$, a **temperature gradient** often forms. This means the outer edges of the substance might reach the melting point and begin to liquefy while the center remains solid. This results in a broad melting range (like the $106\text{--}120\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ seen for substance Y) rather than a precise point.

steps.title: Suggest sample-based improvements
steps.content:
To fix this, we focus on **Sample and Thermal Uniformity**. There are two excellent ways to ensure the whole sample reaches the same temperature at the same time:

1.  **Use a smaller mass:** Reducing the mass (e.g., using a few milligrams in a capillary tube) ensures the heat penetrates the entire sample quickly.
2.  **Crush the sample into a fine powder:** This increases the surface area in contact with the heat source and promotes more uniform heat distribution.

steps.title: State the final improvement
steps.content:
For your answer, you can suggest using a **smaller mass of the substance**. This ensures uniform heating throughout the sample, which allows for a more accurate and sharper melting point determination.

According to the markscheme, suggesting a smaller mass is a valid way to earn the mark for this practical improvement.

highlights.text: using a smaller mass of the substance to ensure uniform heating

steps.title: Define purity and reliability
steps.content: In chemistry, a **pure substance** is characterized by a sharp melting point at a specific temperature. Impure substances typically melt over a range of temperatures. To accurately determine whether substances X, Y, or Z are pure, we must ensure the data collected is reliable and not the result of a one-off error.

highlights.text: determine whether a substance is pure more accurately

steps.title: Suggest repeated trials
steps.content: To increase the **statistical reliability** of the results, the experiment should be **repeated multiple times** for each substance (X, Y, and Z). Instead of a single observation, taking three or more readings allows us to see if the melting behaviour is consistent.

steps.title: Identify and handle anomalies
steps.content:
Repeating the experiment helps identify **anomalies** (outliers). These are data points that differ significantly from others due to random errors, such as a fluctuation in the heat source or a misread thermometer. 

For example, if Substance X melted at $134^{\circ}\text{C}$ in the first trial but $120^{\circ}\text{C}$ in the second, we would know the second trial is likely an anomaly and requires investigation.

ti84.instructions: Enter (134 + 135 + 134) / 3 into the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculating a potential average of three trials for Substance X to show how data consistency is checked.

steps.title: Final improvement suggestion
steps.content:
By verifying the consistency of the data across multiple trials, we can be much more confident in our conclusions. If Substance Y consistently shows a range like $106\text{--}120^{\circ}\text{C}$ across three trials, we have strong statistical evidence that it is impure.

**Improvement:** Repeat the trials for each substance and calculate an average melting point to ensure the findings are consistent and reliable.

parts.content: State the type of bonding present in sodium chloride and in carbon tetrachloride.
parts.markscheme:
- $NaCl$: Ionic bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $CCl_4$: Covalent bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze Sodium Chloride Elements
steps.content:
To determine the bonding in sodium chloride ($NaCl$), we first identify the types of elements involved using the periodic table (Topic S3.1):

- **Sodium ($Na$):** Located in Group 1, it is a metal.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** Located in Group 17, it is a non-metal.

This classification is a prerequisite found in **Structure 3: Classification of matter**.

steps.title: Classify Bonding in $NaCl$
steps.content:
According to the **Elemental Classification** method, a bond formed between a metal and a non-metal is classified as an **ionic bond**. This occurs because the metal atom transfers electrons to the non-metal atom to achieve a stable electron configuration.

$$NaCl: \text{Ionic bonding}$$

steps.title: Analyze Carbon Tetrachloride Elements
steps.content:
Next, we look at the elements in carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$):

- **Carbon ($C$):** A non-metal.
- **Chlorine ($Cl$):** A non-metal.

Identifying these elements correctly is key to using the **covalent model** covered in Topic S2.2.

steps.title: Classify Bonding in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
When two non-metals react, they share electron pairs rather than transferring them. The bond formed between two non-metals is classified as a **covalent bond**.

$$CCl_4: \text{Covalent bonding}$$

steps.title: Use Electronegativity Difference Approach
steps.content:
To determine the bonding type, we use the electronegativity values ($\chi$) from the IB Data Booklet. The difference in electronegativity ($\Delta\chi$) between two atoms tells us if the bond is ionic or covalent.

Generally:
- **$\Delta\chi > 1.8$**: Ionic bonding
- **$\Delta\chi < 1.8$**: Covalent bonding

This concept is part of **Topic S2.4: Bonding as a continuum**.

steps.title: Bonding in Sodium Chloride
steps.content:
Using the Pauling scale in the Data Booklet:
- $\chi(\text{Na}) = 0.9$
- $\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$

Calculation for sodium chloride ($NaCl$):
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 0.9 = 2.3$$

Since $2.3$ is significantly greater than $1.8$, the bonding is **ionic**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 0.9 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference for NaCl.

steps.title: Bonding in Carbon Tetrachloride
steps.content:
Using the Data Booklet:
- $\chi(\text{C}) = 2.6$
- $\chi(\text{Cl}) = 3.2$

Calculation for carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$):
$$\Delta\chi = 3.2 - 2.6 = 0.6$$

Since $0.6$ is much less than $1.8$, the bonding is **covalent**.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3.2 - 2.6 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the electronegativity difference for CCl4.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the electronegativity differences:
- **Sodium chloride:** Ionic bonding
- **Carbon tetrachloride:** Covalent bonding

steps.title: Analyze the experimental setup
steps.content:
The diagram shows a conductivity test involving a battery, a bulb, and electrodes. This setup allows us to deduce bonding types by observing whether a substance conducts electricity in its solid and molten (liquid) states. 

*   **Ionic compounds** conduct electricity when molten because their ions are free to move, but they do not conduct when solid because the ions are locked in a rigid lattice.
*   **Molecular covalent compounds** do not conduct in either state because they consist of neutral molecules with no free-moving charged particles (ions or electrons).

highlights.text: investigate the properties of sodium chloride and carbon tetrachloride in both solid and molten states

steps.title: Deduce bonding for NaCl
steps.content:
Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) is formed from a metal (Sodium) and a non-metal (Chlorine). In this experiment, $NaCl$ would cause the bulb to light up only when molten. This is because melting breaks the strong electrostatic attractions in the ionic lattice, allowing the $Na^{+}$ and $Cl^{-}$ ions to move toward the electrodes. This behavior is the classic signature of **ionic bonding**.

You can find more about this in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Deduce bonding for CCl4
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_{4}$) is composed of two non-metals (Carbon and Chlorine). These atoms share electrons to achieve a full outer shell. In the conductivity test, the bulb would not light up regardless of whether the substance is solid or liquid, because there are no ions or delocalized electrons to carry a charge. This identifies it as having **covalent bonding**.

Review **Topic S2.2: The covalent model** for more details on molecular structures.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Based on their physical properties and the elements involved:

*   **Sodium chloride ($NaCl$):** Ionic bonding
*   **Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_{4}$):** Covalent bonding

parts.content: Explain why sodium chloride does not conduct electricity in the solid state but does when molten.
parts.markscheme:
- In solid $NaCl$, ions are fixed in a lattice and cannot move. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In molten $NaCl$, ions are mobile and can carry charge. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the charge carriers
steps.content: To understand electrical conductivity in sodium chloride ($NaCl$), we first need to identify what carries the charge. $NaCl$ is an ionic compound made of sodium ions ($Na^+$) and chloride ions ($Cl^-$). For a substance to conduct electricity, it must contain particles that are both **charged** and **free to move**.

steps.title: Explain the solid state
steps.content:
In the solid state, $NaCl$ exists as a giant ionic lattice. The $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are held in fixed positions by strong electrostatic forces of attraction. 

Because the ions are **fixed in the lattice** and cannot move from place to place, they cannot carry an electric current. This is why solid sodium chloride does not conduct electricity.

highlights.text: In solid NaCl, ions are fixed in a lattice and cannot move.

steps.title: Explain the molten state
steps.content:
When sodium chloride is heated until it becomes molten (liquid), the high temperature provides enough energy to overcome the strong electrostatic forces holding the lattice together. 

In this state, the lattice structure breaks down, and the **ions become mobile**. Since the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are now free to move throughout the liquid, they can carry the electric charge toward the electrodes, allowing the substance to conduct electricity.

highlights.text: In molten NaCl, ions are mobile and can carry charge.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both states:
1.  **Solid state:** Ions are in fixed positions in a lattice (1 mark).
2.  **Molten state:** Ions are free to move/mobile (1 mark).

This concept is a key part of **Topic S2.1: The ionic model** in your IB Chemistry syllabus.

parts.content: Suggest why carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity in any state.
parts.markscheme: $CCl_4$ has no free-moving ions or electrons. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define electrical conductivity requirements
steps.content:
To understand why a substance conducts (or doesn't conduct) electricity, we use **Charge Carrier Analysis**. For any material to conduct an electric current, it must possess particles that are both **charged** and **mobile** (free to move). These charge carriers are typically either:

1.  **Delocalized electrons** (common in metals and graphite).
2.  **Free-moving ions** (common in molten or aqueous ionic compounds).

steps.title: Analyze the bonding in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a **simple molecular covalent** compound. In this structure, carbon and chlorine atoms are held together by localized covalent bonds where electrons are shared between specific atoms. 

This means:
- There are no ions present because the atoms are not losing or gaining electrons to form charged particles.
- There are no delocalized electrons because all valence electrons are either involved in specific bonds or localized as lone pairs on the chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Apply Charge Carrier Analysis
steps.content:
Since $CCl_4$ consists of neutral molecules, there are no mobile ions to carry charge. Even in the molten (liquid) state, the molecules remain neutral and the electrons remain localized within the covalent bonds. 

Because it lacks both **free-moving ions** and **delocalized electrons**, it cannot conduct electricity in any state.

highlights.text: carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity in any state

steps.title: Final Markscheme Alignment
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, you simply need to state the absence of these carriers.

**Answer:**
$CCl_4$ has no free-moving ions or electrons.

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content: To understand why a substance conducts or doesn't conduct electricity, we first look at its bonding. Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a **simple covalent molecular substance**. It is formed from two non-metals (carbon and chlorine) sharing electrons to form covalent bonds.

steps.title: Analyze the charge carriers
steps.content:
For electricity to flow, a substance must have **mobile charged particles**, such as free-moving ions or delocalized electrons. 

In $CCl_4$:
1. The substance consists of **neutral molecules**. There are no ions present in the solid or liquid (molten) state.
2. The valence electrons are **localized** within the specific covalent bonds between the carbon and chlorine atoms.

steps.title: Relate structure to conductivity
steps.content:
Because the molecules are neutral and the electrons are held tightly within the bonds, there are no charged particles available to move and carry a current. 

This is why $CCl_4$ remains a non-conductor in any state, unlike ionic compounds (like $NaCl$) which can conduct when molten because their ions become free to move.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you simply need to state that the substance lacks the necessary mobile charges.

**Final Answer:** Carbon tetrachloride does not conduct electricity because it has **no free-moving (mobile) ions or electrons**.

parts.content: Use the diagram and your knowledge of structure and bonding to deduce which substance(s) contain mobile ions.
parts.markscheme: Only molten $NaCl$ contains mobile ions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the bonding types
steps.content:
To determine which substance has mobile ions, we first need to look at their bonding. 

*   **Sodium chloride ($NaCl$)** is an **ionic compound** formed between a metal (Na) and a non-metal (Cl). It consists of a giant lattice of positive sodium ions ($Na^+$) and negative chloride ions ($Cl^-$).
*   **Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$)** is a **covalent molecular compound** formed between two non-metals. It consists of neutral molecules held together by weak intermolecular forces.

This fundamental difference is covered in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model** and **Structure 2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Evaluate ion mobility
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the state of these substances. 

In **solid $NaCl$**, the ions are locked into a rigid 3D lattice by strong electrostatic forces. Because they are in fixed positions, they cannot move to carry a charge. However, when $NaCl$ is **molten** (melted), the lattice structure breaks down, and the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions are free to move throughout the liquid.

In contrast, $CCl_4$ is made of neutral molecules. Whether it is solid or molten, it does not contain ions at all, only molecules.

steps.title: Deduce the final answer
steps.content:
Based on our analysis:
1. $CCl_4$ has no ions in either state.
2. Solid $NaCl$ has ions, but they are not mobile.
3. **Molten $NaCl$** has ions that are free to move.

Therefore, the only substance containing mobile ions is molten $NaCl$.

steps.title: Analyze $CCl_4$ properties
steps.content: First, let's look at carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). It is a covalent molecular substance composed of neutral molecules. Because it does not contain any ions in either its solid or molten state, we can eliminate it immediately from the list of substances containing mobile ions.

steps.title: Evaluate solid $NaCl$
steps.content: Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) is an ionic compound containing $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. However, in its solid state, these ions are locked in a rigid 3D lattice structure. While the ions exist, they are not **mobile** because they are held firmly in place by strong electrostatic attractions.

steps.title: Identify the mobile phase
steps.content: When $NaCl$ is heated to its melting point to become molten, the giant ionic lattice breaks down. This allows the $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions to move freely throughout the liquid. Therefore, only molten $NaCl$ contains ions that are actually mobile.

steps.title: Final Deduction
steps.content:
By ruling out the molecular substance ($CCl_4$) and the fixed lattice structure (solid $NaCl$), we conclude that only molten $NaCl$ fits the criteria.

**Final Answer:** Only molten $NaCl$ contains mobile ions.

parts.content: State and explain the difference in melting point between sodium chloride and carbon tetrachloride.
parts.markscheme:
- $NaCl$ has a much higher melting point than $CCl_4$. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Due to strong electrostatic forces between ions in a lattice vs. weak London forces between $CCl_4$ molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: State the difference
steps.content: Sodium chloride ($NaCl$) has a significantly higher melting point than carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). To understand why, we need to look at how their atoms are held together in the solid state.

highlights.text: NaCl has a much higher melting point than CCl4

steps.title: Analyze sodium chloride structure
steps.content:
Sodium chloride is an ionic compound that exists as a **giant ionic lattice**. In this structure, ions are held together by **strong electrostatic attractions** between oppositely charged $Na^+$ and $Cl^-$ ions. 

Because these attractions are very strong and extend throughout the entire 3D lattice, a large amount of thermal energy is required to overcome them and melt the solid.

highlights.text: strong electrostatic forces between ions in a lattice

steps.title: Analyze carbon tetrachloride structure
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride is a **simple molecular** substance. While the $C-Cl$ bonds within the molecule are strong covalent bonds, the forces holding the $CCl_4$ molecules to each other in a solid are **weak London dispersion forces** (intermolecular forces).

Since $CCl_4$ is a non-polar molecule, these London forces are the only attractions present between molecules.

highlights.text: weak London forces between CCl4 molecules

steps.title: Contrast the energy required
steps.content:
The difference in melting point comes down to the energy needed to disrupt these different types of forces:

1.  In **$NaCl$**, you must break strong ionic bonds within a giant lattice.
2.  In **$CCl_4$**, you only need to overcome weak intermolecular forces between molecules.

Therefore, $NaCl$ requires much more energy to melt, resulting in a much higher melting point. This comparison is a core concept in **Topic S2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

parts.content: Explain why carbon tetrachloride is a liquid at room temperature. Justify your answer based on its intermolecular forces.
parts.markscheme:
- Weak London dispersion forces exist between molecules. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The forces are between non-polar molecules / insufficient energy to overcome forces at room temperature. <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze molecular polarity
steps.content:
To determine the intermolecular forces, we first look at the structure of carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$). Although the individual $C-Cl$ bonds are polar due to the difference in electronegativity, the molecule has a **symmetrical tetrahedral shape**. 

Because of this symmetry, the bond dipoles cancel each other out, making the $CCl_4$ molecule **non-polar**. This is a key prerequisite concept from **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Since $CCl_4$ is a non-polar molecule, the only type of intermolecular forces it can form are **London dispersion forces** (also known as instantaneous induced dipole-induced dipole forces).

These forces occur because of the constant movement of electrons, which creates temporary dipoles that induce dipoles in neighboring molecules.

steps.title: Explain the liquid state
steps.content:
At room temperature (approximately $298\text{ K}$), the molecules have a certain amount of kinetic energy. For $CCl_4$, this energy is **insufficient to completely overcome** the London dispersion forces between the molecules. 

Because the forces are strong enough to keep the molecules close together but weak enough to allow them to slide past each other, the substance exists as a **liquid** rather than a gas. 

**Markscheme Note:** 
- Mentioning **London dispersion forces** earns the first mark.
- Stating that the molecules are **non-polar** or that there is **insufficient energy to overcome these forces** at room temperature earns the second mark.

steps.title: Identify the intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$) is a non-polar molecule because of its symmetrical tetrahedral shape. Because it is non-polar, the only intermolecular forces acting between its molecules are weak **London dispersion forces** (also known as van der Waals forces or induced dipole-induced dipole forces).

This is the first key point needed to earn marks according to the markscheme.

highlights.text: carbon tetrachloride is a liquid at room temperature

steps.title: Apply energy-state relationship
steps.content:
The state of matter is determined by the balance between the thermal (kinetic) energy of the particles and the strength of the intermolecular forces holding them together. 

At room temperature (approximately $298\text{ K}$), the average kinetic energy provided to the molecules is:
1.  **Enough** to overcome the rigid lattice structure of a solid, allowing the molecules to move and flow.
2.  **Insufficient** to completely overcome the London dispersion forces, meaning the molecules remain close together rather than flying apart as a gas.

Because the energy is enough to overcome some but not all of the forces, $CCl_4$ exists as a **liquid**.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on an IB exam, your answer should connect these two points:

*   **Mark 1:** State that $CCl_4$ molecules are held together by weak London dispersion forces.
*   **Mark 2:** Explain that at room temperature, there is insufficient energy to fully overcome these forces to form a gas, resulting in the liquid state.

This concept is covered in **Structure 2.2.9: Intermolecular forces and physical properties**.

steps.title: Analyze bond polarity
steps.content: To understand the properties of carbon tetrachloride ($CCl_4$), we first look at the individual bonds. Chlorine is more electronegative than carbon, which creates a polar covalent bond where the electron density is pulled towards the chlorine atoms. This creates individual bond dipoles directed from $C$ to $Cl$.

steps.title: Evaluate molecular symmetry
steps.content: According to VSEPR theory (Topic S2.2.4), carbon has four bonding electron domains and no lone pairs, resulting in a **tetrahedral** geometry. Because all four atoms surrounding the central carbon are identical (chlorine), the molecule is perfectly symmetrical.

steps.title: Determine molecular polarity
steps.content: Due to the tetrahedral symmetry, the individual polar bond dipoles cancel each other out exactly. As a result, $CCl_4$ is a **non-polar molecule**.

steps.title: Identify intermolecular forces
steps.content:
Because the molecules are non-polar, the only attractions between them are **weak London dispersion forces**. These forces arise from temporary induced dipoles. 

In the IB markscheme, identifying these forces earns the first mark.

highlights.text: Weak London dispersion forces exist between molecules.

steps.title: Relate forces to state
steps.content:
At room temperature, there is insufficient thermal energy to completely overcome these intermolecular forces to turn the substance into a gas, but the forces are weak enough to allow the molecules to slide past one another. Therefore, $CCl_4$ exists as a liquid.

Stating that these forces occur between non-polar molecules earns the second mark.

highlights.text: The forces are between non-polar molecules / insufficient energy to overcome forces at room temperature.

parts.content: Draw the Lewis (electron dot) structure for the $Na^+$ ion and a $CCl_4$ molecule
parts.markscheme:
$Na^+$: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost) <marks>1 mark</marks>

$CCl_4$: carbon single bonded to four $Cl$ atoms; each $Cl$ with 3 lone pairs <marks>1 mark</marks>


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7e8a691d-b87a-4c65-9943-1ee90d7d96eb)

steps.title: Analyze $Na^+$ ion structure
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) is a Group 1 element with 1 valence electron. To form the $Na^+$ ion, it loses this single electron to achieve a stable noble gas configuration (matching Neon). 

In IB chemistry, when a metal ion loses all its valence electrons, the Lewis structure is drawn by simply showing the element's symbol and its charge. No dots are placed around it because the original valence shell is now empty.

$$Na^+$$

steps.title: Count $CCl_4$ valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw the $CCl_4$ molecule, we first calculate the total number of valence electrons available:

- Carbon ($C$) is in Group 14: **4 electrons**
- Chlorine ($Cl$) is in Group 17: **7 electrons per atom**

$$\text{Total} = 4 + (4 \times 7) = 32 \text{ electrons}$$

gdc.instructions: Input the sum of valence electrons.

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to find the total.

calculator.description: Calculate total valence electrons for carbon tetrachloride.

steps.title: Distribute electrons in $CCl_4$
steps.content:
Now we systematically distribute the 32 electrons:

1.  **Form the skeleton**: Place $C$ in the center and connect the four $Cl$ atoms with single bonds. Each bond uses 2 electrons, so $4 \times 2 = 8$ electrons used.
2.  **Complete octets**: Subtract the bonding electrons from the total ($32 - 8 = 24$). Place these 24 electrons as lone pairs around the $Cl$ atoms. Each $Cl$ needs 6 more electrons (3 lone pairs) to complete its octet.
3.  **Check Carbon**: The central Carbon already has 4 bonds (8 electrons), so its octet is satisfied.

Every atom now has a full valence shell using exactly 32 electrons.

ti84.instructions: Subtract bonding electrons from total.

calculator.description: Verify remaining electrons for lone pairs.

steps.title: Final Lewis Structures
steps.content:
Combining the steps, your final diagrams should look like this:

- **$Na^+$**: Just the symbol and the charge: $$Na^+$$
- **$CCl_4$**: A central $C$ with four single bonds to $Cl$, and each $Cl$ surrounded by 6 dots (3 lone pairs).

$$\begin{matrix} & & :\ddot{Cl}: & & \\ & & | & & \\ :\ddot{Cl} & - & C & - & \ddot{Cl}: \\ & & | & & \\ & & :\ddot{Cl}: & & \end{matrix}$$

highlights.text: Na+: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost)
CCl_4: carbon single bonded to four Cl atoms; each Cl with 3 lone pairs

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To draw Lewis structures, we first determine the number of valence electrons for each atom using their group numbers in the periodic table:

*   **Sodium (Na)**: Group 1, so it has 1 valence electron.
*   **Carbon (C)**: Group 14, so it has 4 valence electrons.
*   **Chlorine (Cl)**: Group 17, so it has 7 valence electrons.

This is a fundamental concept from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

steps.title: Draw the Sodium Ion
steps.content:
Sodium ($Na$) forms an ion by losing its single valence electron to achieve a stable, full inner shell. 

In a Lewis structure for a positive ion (cation), we show the symbol and the charge. Since the original third-shell electron is gone, the diagram shows no dots or crosses around the symbol.

$$[Na]^+$$

highlights.text: Na^+: symbol only, no electrons shown (full outer shell lost)

steps.title: Bonding in Carbon Tetrachloride
steps.content:
For $CCl_4$, Carbon is the central atom. It uses its 4 valence electrons (represented as crosses $\times$) to form four single covalent bonds with four Chlorine atoms.

Each Chlorine atom provides 1 electron (represented as a dot $\bullet$) to share with Carbon, creating four bonding pairs. This allows Carbon to reach a stable octet (8 electrons).

$$\begin{matrix} & \text{Cl} & \\ \text{Cl} & \text{C} & \text{Cl} \\ & \text{Cl} & \end{matrix}$$

highlights.text: carbon single bonded to four Cl atoms

steps.title: Complete Chlorine's lone pairs
steps.content:
Each Chlorine atom starts with 7 valence electrons. Since it shares only 1 electron with Carbon, it has 6 non-bonding electrons remaining. These must be drawn as **3 lone pairs** (pairs of dots $\bullet \bullet$) around each Chlorine atom.

This ensures every Chlorine atom also satisfies the octet rule ($2$ shared $+ 6$ non-bonding $= 8$ electrons).

**Final Dot and Cross Representation:**
$$\begin{array}{ccc} & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \\ \bullet \bullet & \times \bullet & \bullet \bullet \\ \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet & \bullet \text{C} \times & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ \bullet \bullet & \bullet \times & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \\ & \bullet \text{Cl} \bullet \\ & \bullet \bullet \end{array}$$

questions.specification: A student is given a mixture of sodium chloride and sand. Which sequence of separation techniques would be most appropriate?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.1 Introduction to the particulate nature of matter > S1.1.1 Elements, Compounds and Mixtures
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.1.1 Elements, Compounds and Mixtures
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is about separating a mixture (sodium chloride and sand) using physical separation techniques, which falls under mixtures and their separation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.2 The covalent model > S2.2.10 Chromatography
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.2.10 Chromatography
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Chromatography appears as a distractor option, but the question is broadly about choosing separation methods; however chromatography is not the appropriate technique here.

options.content: Filtration, then evaporation
options.markscheme: Sodium chloride is soluble in water, whereas sand is insoluble. After adding water to the mixture, filtration separates the insoluble sand as the residue. Subsequent evaporation of the filtrate (the salt solution) leaves the sodium chloride.

options.content: Distillation, then filtration
options.markscheme: Distillation is used to separate liquids with different boiling points or a solvent from a solution. It is not appropriate for separating two solids like salt and sand.

options.content: Chromatography, then evaporation
options.markscheme: Chromatography is used to separate components of a mixture based on their different affinities for stationary and mobile phases (e.g., pigments in ink). It is not used for salt and sand.

options.content: Evaporation, then filtration
options.markscheme: If the mixture is evaporated before filtration, the sand would remain mixed with the salt. Filtration must occur first to remove the insoluble component (sand) before the soluble component (salt) is recovered through evaporation.

questions.specification:
A student investigates the solubility of potassium nitrate ($\text{KNO}_3$) in water at different temperatures. The results are shown in the table below.

**Table 1. Solubility of potassium nitrate in water**

| Temperature ($^\circ\text{C}$) | Mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved in $100\text{ g}$ water (g) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 10 | 21 |
| 20 | 31 |
| 30 | 45 |
| 40 | 63 |
| 50 | 85 |
| 60 | 109 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 2. Models of bonding and structure > S2.1 The ionic model > S2.1.3 Lattice structures and properties
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.1.3 Lattice structures and properties
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Question centers on solubility of an ionic compound (KNO3) in water and how it changes with temperature, including explaining why excess solid remains (saturated solution) and why solubility generally increases with temperature—linked to ionic lattice vs hydration/solution energetics.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Structure 1. Models of the particulate nature of matter > S1.1 Introduction to the particulate nature of matter > S1.1.3 Temperature and Kinetic Energy
labeledSubSubTopics.title: S1.1.3 Temperature and Kinetic Energy

parts.content: Outline the trend shown in the data.
parts.markscheme: Solubility increases as temperature increases. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the temperature change
steps.content: To identify a trend, we first look at how the independent variable changes. In the first column, we can see that the **Temperature** values are consistently increasing from $10^\circ\text{C}$ up to $60^\circ\text{C}$.

steps.title: Analyze the mass change
steps.content: Next, we look at the corresponding values in the second column. As we move down the table, the **Mass of $\text{KNO}_3$** dissolved also increases ($21 \rightarrow 31 \rightarrow 45 \dots$). This shows a direct numerical relationship where both values move in the same direction.

highlights.text: Mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved in $100\text{ g}$ water (g)

steps.title: Outline the trend
steps.content:
Since both variables increase together, we can state the trend clearly: as the temperature increases, the solubility (mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved) also increases.

According to the markscheme, stating that **solubility increases as temperature increases** earns the mark.

steps.title: Identify the variables
steps.content:
To outline a trend, we first need to identify our independent and dependent variables from the data provided. 

*   **Independent Variable ($x$-axis):** Temperature ($^\circ\text{C}$)
*   **Dependent Variable ($y$-axis):** Solubility (Mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ in $100\text{ g}$ water)

In this experiment, the student is changing the temperature to see how it affects the amount of solute that can dissolve.

highlights.text: Mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved in $100\text{ g}$ water (g)

steps.title: Visualize the data points
steps.content:
A great way to identify trends is to visualize the data on a coordinate plane. Let's look at the pairs of coordinates $(T, S)$:

$$(10, 21), (20, 31), (30, 45), (40, 63), (50, 85), (60, 109)$$

If you were to sketch these points, you would see that as you move to the right along the $x$-axis (increasing temperature), the points move higher up the $y$-axis (increasing mass dissolved).

calculator.description: Graphing the solubility data to visualize the trend.

steps.title: Analyze the slope
steps.content:
When we connect these points or draw a line of best fit, we see a curve with a **positive slope**. In graphical terms, a positive slope indicates a direct relationship between the two variables.

Mathematically, for any two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ where $x_2 > x_1$, we find that $y_2 > y_1$. For example, comparing $20^\circ\text{C}$ and $30^\circ\text{C}$:

$$30 > 20 \implies 45 > 31$$

This confirmed upward direction shows that the two variables change in the same direction.

steps.title: State the final trend
steps.content:
Based on our graphical interpretation, as the temperature increases, the solubility (mass of $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolved) also increases.

**Final Answer:**
Solubility increases as temperature increases.

parts.content: Calculate the average rate of increase in solubility between $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ in $\text{g } ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Solubility at $50\ ^\circ\text{C} = 85\text{ g}$ and solubility at $20\ ^\circ\text{C} = 31\text{ g}$
- Difference $= 54\text{ g}$ over $30\ ^\circ\text{C}$ <marks>1</marks>
- Rate $= 54 \div 30 = 1.8\ \text{g } ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify solubility values
steps.content:
To find the average rate of increase, we first need to extract the solubility values for the two temperatures mentioned in the question from **Table 1**.

*   At $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the solubility is $31\text{ g}$.
*   At $50\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the solubility is $85\text{ g}$.

steps.title: Calculate the changes
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the change in solubility ($Δ\text{Mass}$) and the change in temperature ($Δ\text{Temperature}$) over this range.

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta\text{Mass} &= 85\text{ g} - 31\text{ g} = 54\text{ g} \\ \Delta\text{Temperature} &= 50\ ^\circ\text{C} - 20\ ^\circ\text{C} = 30\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned}$$

Identifying these differences correctly earns the first mark on the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate the average rate
steps.content:
The average rate of increase is found by dividing the change in solubility by the change in temperature:

$$\text{Average Rate} = \frac{\Delta\text{Mass}}{\Delta\text{Temperature}}$$

$$\text{Average Rate} = \frac{54\text{ g}}{30\ ^\circ\text{C}} = 1.8\ \text{g } ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 54 divided by 30 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the final rate of increase.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The average rate of increase in solubility between $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is **$1.8\ \text{g } ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$**.

This calculation aligns with **Topic R2.2: How fast? The rate of chemical change**, where rates are defined as the change in a property over the change in time (or, in this case, temperature).

A student stirs $40\text{ g}$ of $\text{KNO}_3$ into $100\text{ g}$ of water at $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$. 

Predict what will be observed after the solution is allowed to stand, and explain your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Some $\text{KNO}_3$ will remain undissolved / crystals will be visible. <marks>1</marks>
- Because solubility at $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is only $31\text{ g}$ per $100\text{ g}$ water, so $40\text{ g}$ exceeds this amount / the solution is saturated. <marks>1</marks>

steps.title: Identify solubility at 20 °C
steps.content:
First, we look at the provided data table to find the maximum amount of $\text{KNO}_3$ that can dissolve in $100\text{ g}$ of water at the specific temperature of $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

From **Table 1**, we can see that at $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$, the solubility is $31\text{ g}$ per $100\text{ g}$ of water.

steps.title: Compare added mass to solubility
steps.content:
Next, we compare the amount the student actually added to the maximum solubility limit:

- Amount added: $40\text{ g}$
- Maximum solubility at $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$: $31\text{ g}$

Since $40\text{ g} > 31\text{ g}$, the student has added more $\text{KNO}_3$ than the water can physically dissolve at this temperature.

ti84.instructions: Enter 40 - 31 on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the mass of undissolved KNO3.

steps.title: Predict and explain observation
steps.content:
Because the amount added exceeds the solubility limit, the solution is **saturated**. Any amount added beyond the limit will not dissolve and will remain as a solid.

**Observation:** Solid crystals of $\text{KNO}_3$ will be seen at the bottom of the container.

**Explanation:** The solution is saturated because $40\text{ g}$ exceeds the maximum solubility of $31\text{ g}$ per $100\text{ g}$ of water at $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Approximately $9\text{ g}$ of $\text{KNO}_3$ will remain undissolved.

highlights.text: Some KNO3 will remain undissolved / crystals will be visible.
solubility at 20 °C is only 31 g per 100 g water

parts.content: Suggest one reason why solubility increases with temperature for most ionic compounds.
parts.markscheme:
More energy is available to break ionic bonds / disrupt the lattice and hydrate ions. <marks>1</marks>

<note>Accept: increased kinetic energy leads to more frequent collisions or more effective disruption of the solid structure.</note>

steps.title: Temperature and Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
According to the kinetic molecular theory, temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of particles. As the temperature of the water increases, both the water molecules and the ions in the $\text{KNO}_3$ lattice gain more kinetic energy.

You can review this fundamental concept in **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**.

steps.title: Energy to Break the Lattice
steps.content:
In an ionic solid like $\text{KNO}_3$, ions are held together by strong electrostatic forces in a regular 3D arrangement called a lattice. To dissolve the solid, energy must be supplied to overcome this lattice enthalpy and break the ionic bonds.

$$\text{KNO}_3(s) \rightarrow \text{K}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$$ 

This process is explained in **Topic S2.1.3: Lattice structures and properties**.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Because the particles have higher kinetic energy at higher temperatures, there is more energy available to disrupt the lattice and hydrate the ions. This allows more $\text{KNO}_3$ to transition into the aqueous phase, which is why we see the mass dissolved increase in the table.

highlights.text: More energy is available to break ionic bonds / disrupt the lattice and hydrate ions.

steps.title: Model as an equilibrium
steps.content:
To understand why solubility changes, we can treat the dissolution of an ionic compound like potassium nitrate ($\text{KNO}_3$) as a dynamic equilibrium between the solid and its dissolved ions:

$$\text{KNO}_3(s) \rightleftharpoons \text{K}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$$

This process is covered in **Topic R2.3: How far? The extent of chemical change**.

steps.title: Identify the thermal process
steps.content:
For most ionic compounds, the process of dissolving is **endothermic**. This means that energy (heat) must be absorbed from the surroundings to overcome the strong electrostatic forces holding the ionic lattice together.

Using the standard enthalpy formula from your data booklet:

$$\Delta H^{\ominus} = \Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ products}) - \Sigma(\Delta H_{\mathrm{f}}^{\ominus} \text{ reactants})$$

If the reaction is endothermic, we can essentially think of 'heat' as a reactant:

$$\text{KNO}_3(s) + \text{heat} \rightleftharpoons \text{K}^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq)$$ 

This relates to the markscheme's point that more energy is required to disrupt the solid structure.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier’s Principle**, if a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in temperature, the system will shift its equilibrium position to oppose that change.

When we increase the temperature, we are adding 'heat' to the system. To oppose this, the system will shift in the direction that consumes heat—the **endothermic direction** (to the right).

steps.title: Relate to increased solubility
steps.content:
Because the equilibrium shifts to the right (the aqueous side), more solid $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolves into the water. This explains why the mass of dissolved salt increases as temperature goes up.

Looking at the data provided, we can see this effect clearly: as the temperature increases from $10^\circ\text{C}$ to $60^\circ\text{C}$, the mass dissolved increases from $21\text{ g}$ to $109\text{ g}$.

steps.title: Introduce Gibbs Free Energy
steps.content:
To understand why solubility increases with temperature, we look at the **Gibbs Free Energy equation** from your data booklet. For a process like dissolution to be spontaneous (occur on its own), the change in Gibbs Free Energy ($ΔG$) must be negative.

$$\Delta G^{\ominus}=\Delta H^{\ominus}-T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

steps.title: Evaluate Entropy Change
steps.content:
When an ionic solid like $\text{KNO}_3$ dissolves, the highly ordered crystal lattice breaks down into mobile ions in solution. This transition from a solid to an aqueous state significantly increases the disorder of the system.

Since disorder increases, the **entropy change ($ΔS$) is positive** (${\Delta S > 0}$). This concept is covered in **Topic R1.4: Entropy and spontaneity**.

steps.title: Analyze Temperature Effect
steps.content:
Now, let's look at the temperature term in the equation: $-T \Delta S$. 

Because $\Delta S$ is positive, the term $-T \Delta S$ becomes more negative as the temperature ($T$) increases. Most ionic dissolutions are endothermic (${\Delta H > 0}$), meaning they require energy to break the lattice. 

At higher temperatures, the larger negative value of $-T \Delta S$ helps to 'offset' the positive $\Delta H$, making $\Delta G$ more negative.

steps.title: State the Final Reason
steps.content:
Increasing the temperature makes the dissolution process more **thermodynamically favorable** (more spontaneous). 

Essentially, the increase in entropy during dissolution allows the system to overcome the energy barrier required to break the ionic bonds in the lattice more effectively at higher temperatures, leading to increased solubility.

parts.content: State the definition of a pure substance in terms of the particulate model.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that a pure substance contains only one type of particle throughout <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the particulate model
steps.content: In chemistry, the **particulate model of matter** suggests that all substances are made of tiny, discrete particles. To define a pure substance, we look at whether these particles are identical or different throughout the material. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**.

steps.title: Define a pure substance
steps.content:
By direct definition, a **pure substance** is a form of matter that consists of **only one type of particle** throughout its entire structure.

These particles can be atoms (in an element like Helium), molecules (in a compound like distilled water, $H_2O$), or formula units (in a compound like salt, $NaCl$).

highlights.text: only one type of particle throughout

steps.title: Final Statement
steps.content:
To earn the mark, your answer must emphasize the uniformity of the particles. 

**Final Answer:** A pure substance contains only one type of chemical particle throughout.

parts.content: Describe two observable differences between a physical change and a chemical change.
parts.markscheme:
Award [1] for each of any two of the following:
- **Describe** physical changes as usually reversible and chemical changes as often irreversible <marks>A1</marks>
- **Describe** physical changes as not forming new substances and chemical changes as forming new substances <marks>A1</marks>
- **Describe** physical changes as involving changes in state or appearance and chemical changes as involving colour change, gas formation, or precipitate formation <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify comparison criteria
steps.content: To distinguish between a physical and a chemical change, we can use the **Direct Descriptive Comparison** method. We will compare them based on two main criteria: the **identity of the substances** involved and the **reversibility** of the process.

steps.title: Compare substance identity
steps.content:
The first major difference is whether the chemical makeup changes:

*   **Physical Change:** No new substances are formed. The particles remain the same, even if they move further apart or change state (e.g., liquid water $H_2O$ becoming steam $H_2O$).
*   **Chemical Change:** New chemical substances are formed. The atoms are rearranged to create products with entirely different chemical properties than the reactants.

highlights.text: Describe physical changes as not forming new substances and chemical changes as forming new substances

steps.title: Compare reversibility
steps.content:
The second difference relates to how easily the change can be undone:

*   **Physical Change:** These are usually **reversible**. For example, you can melt ice into water and then freeze it back into ice again quite easily.
*   **Chemical Change:** These are often **irreversible** (or very difficult to reverse) because they involve the breaking and forming of strong chemical bonds. For example, you cannot easily turn wood ash and smoke back into a piece of wood.

highlights.text: Describe physical changes as usually reversible and chemical changes as often irreversible

steps.title: Final Review
steps.content:
By providing these two distinct descriptions, you satisfy the requirements for both marks:

1.  **Identity:** Physical changes do not form new substances, whereas chemical changes do.
2.  **Reversibility:** Physical changes are usually reversible, whereas chemical changes are often irreversible.

(Note: You could also mention observable cues like gas formation or color changes as a third alternative difference!)

parts.content:
A student heats a sample of naphthalene (mothballs). Initially, the solid is white and crystalline. Upon heating, it directly converts into a gas, skipping the liquid phase.

State the name of this change of state.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the name of this change of state is sublimation <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the phase transition
steps.content:
The question describes a process where naphthalene moves from one state of matter to another without passing through the intermediate liquid phase. We are looking for the transition:

$$\text{solid} \rightarrow \text{gas}$$

highlights.text: directly converts into a gas, skipping the liquid phase

steps.title: Apply standard nomenclature
steps.content:
In chemistry, and specifically under **Topic S1.1.2: The Kinetic Molecular Theory**, we learn specific names for each change of state. 

*   **Melting**: solid to liquid
*   **Boiling/Evaporation**: liquid to gas
*   **Sublimation**: solid directly to gas

Since the naphthalene skips the liquid phase, this is a clear case of direct identification.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: According to the markscheme, the correct term for this specific phase change is **sublimation**.

highlights.text: State that the name of this change of state is sublimation

parts.content: Suggest why this is considered a physical change rather than a chemical change.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that it is a physical change because no new substances are formed or only the state of matter changes <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Define substance identity
steps.content: To determine if a change is physical or chemical, we look at the identity of the substances involved. In a **physical change**, the chemical composition of the substance remains exactly the same before and after the process.

steps.title: Analyze the process
steps.content: A chemical change requires the breaking and forming of chemical bonds to create entirely new substances with different chemical formulas. Since this process (such as a change of state) only alters the arrangement or energy of the particles without changing the molecules themselves, no new chemical products are formed.

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content: Because the chemical identity of the substance is preserved and no new substances are produced, the change is classified as a physical change.

steps.title: Define the change in state
steps.content:
To determine why a process is a physical change, we look at what is happening to the matter at a molecular level. In a physical change, the **state of matter** (solid, liquid, or gas) or the phase of the substance is altered, but its chemical identity remains exactly the same.

This is a core concept in **Topic S1.1: Introduction to the particulate nature of matter**, where we learn that physical changes are often reversible and involve changes in the arrangement of particles rather than their composition.

steps.title: Analyze chemical identity
steps.content:
Using the **State of Matter Approach**, we observe that the substance undergoes a transition (such as melting, boiling, or dissolving) where the molecules themselves do not break apart or rearrange into different molecules. 

Since the chemical formula of the substance stays the same throughout the process, we can conclude that **no new substances are formed**.

steps.title: Final justification for marks
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must explicitly state that the identity of the substance is preserved. You can phrase your answer in one of two ways:
1. Only the **state of matter** (or phase) changes.
2. No **new substances** are formed during the process.

Both points highlight that the internal chemical structure remains constant, which is the defining characteristic of a physical change.

highlights.text: State that it is a physical change because no new substances are formed or only the state of matter changes

parts.content:
A sample of matter contains water and ethanol, which are completely miscible. The student is asked to obtain pure ethanol from the mixture.

Identify the method used to separate this mixture.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the method used to separate the mixture as (fractional) distillation <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Analyze the mixture properties
steps.content:
First, let's look at the components of the mixture. The question specifies that we have a sample of **water and ethanol** and that they are **completely miscible**. 

In chemistry, "miscible" means the two liquids dissolve in each other in all proportions, forming a single homogeneous phase. Because they are fully mixed, we cannot use mechanical separation methods like filtration or a separating funnel (which only works for immiscible liquids like oil and water).

steps.title: Identify the boiling points
steps.content:
To separate miscible liquids, we exploit differences in their physical properties. The most significant difference between water and ethanol is their **boiling point**:

- Ethanol has a boiling point of approximately $78.37 ^\circ\text{C}$.
- Water has a boiling point of $100.00 ^\circ\text{C}$.

When we heat the mixture, the component with the lower boiling point (ethanol) will vaporize more readily than the component with the higher boiling point (water).

steps.title: Select the separation method
steps.content:
Since we have two miscible liquids with different boiling points, the standard laboratory method for separation is **distillation**. 

Because their boiling points are relatively close (less than $100 ^\circ\text{C}$ apart), **fractional distillation** is the most effective technique. This involves using a fractionating column to allow for multiple condensation-evaporation cycles, ensuring the ethanol obtained is as pure as possible.

parts.content: Explain how this method works using the idea of differences in physical properties.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the method separates substances based on differences in boiling points <marks>M1</marks>
- **Explain** that ethanol has a lower boiling point than water, so it evaporates first and is then condensed and collected <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the physical property
steps.content:
To understand how distillation (the method described) works, we first need to identify the specific physical property that differs between the substances. 

Distillation separates the components of a liquid mixture based on their different **boiling points**. This is the temperature at which a liquid's vapor pressure equals the external pressure, causing it to turn into a gas.

steps.title: Compare ethanol and water
steps.content:
In this specific mixture, **ethanol has a lower boiling point** than water. 

Because ethanol molecules have slightly weaker overall intermolecular forces compared to water molecules, ethanol is more **volatile**. This means it requires less thermal energy to overcome those forces and transition from the liquid phase to the gaseous (vapor) phase.

steps.title: The separation mechanism
steps.content:
When the mixture is heated, the following sequence occurs:

1.  **Evaporation:** The substance with the lower boiling point (ethanol) evaporates first.
2.  **Condensation:** The ethanol vapor rises, enters the condenser, and is cooled down.
3.  **Collection:** The cooling causes the vapor to condense back into a liquid, which is then collected in a separate flask.

Water, having a higher boiling point, remains in the original heating flask, effectively separating the two liquids.

steps.title: Recall KMT basics
steps.content: The Kinetic Molecular Theory (KMT) provides the theoretical basis for the ideal gas law ($$PV = nRT$$). It consists of several postulates that simplify the complex behavior of real gases into a predictable model. This concept is foundational to **Topic S1.1.2: The Kinetic Molecular Theory** and **Topic S1.5.1: The ideal gas model**.

highlights.text: State the assumptions of the kinetic molecular theory as it applies to gases.

steps.title: Space and movement
steps.content:
The first two assumptions describe how gas particles occupy space and move within it:

1.  **Constant, random motion**: Gas particles are in continuous motion, moving in straight lines in all directions until they collide.
2.  **Negligible volume**: The particles themselves are considered to have zero volume compared to the total volume of the container (they are treated as point masses).

steps.title: Interaction and energy
steps.content:
The remaining assumptions describe how the particles interact with one another:

1.  **No intermolecular forces**: There are no forces of attraction or repulsion between the gas particles (except during collisions).
2.  **Perfectly elastic collisions**: When particles collide with each other or the container walls, there is no net loss of kinetic energy.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, stating any **two** of these points correctly will earn you full marks for this question. 

$$\text{Total Marks} = 1\text{ (Assumption A)} + 1\text{ (Assumption B)} = 2$$

parts.content: Explain why the temperature of a substance remains constant during a change of state, even though heat is still being added.
parts.markscheme:
- Energy added is used to overcome intermolecular forces, not to increase kinetic energy. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Hence, temperature remains constant during the phase change. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Relate temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
To understand this process, we first look at the definition of temperature from the **Kinetic Molecular Theory (Topic S1.1.2)**. Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the particles in a substance. 

If the average speed of the particles does not change, the temperature will not change.

steps.title: Energy and intermolecular forces
steps.content:
When heat is added during a change of state (such as melting or boiling), that energy is not used to increase the speed of the particles. Instead, the energy is used to overcome the **intermolecular forces** holding the particles in their current phase. 

In terms of energy types, this added thermal energy increases the **potential energy** ($E_p$) of the system as the particles move further apart against their attractive forces.

highlights.text: Energy added is used to overcome intermolecular forces, not to increase kinetic energy.

steps.title: Conclusion on constant temperature
steps.content:
Because the added energy is entirely consumed by the increase in potential energy (breaking or weakening bonds) and does not increase the average kinetic energy ($E_k$), the temperature remains constant.

This plateau on a heating curve lasts until the phase change is complete and all intermolecular forces for that state have been sufficiently overcome.

highlights.text: Hence, temperature remains constant during the phase change.
