parts.content: State one difference between a compound and a mixture.
parts.markscheme:
A compound has components that are chemically bonded; a mixture has components that are physically combined.
<marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Identify the classification of matter
steps.content: To answer this question, we look at **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures**. We need to distinguish between these two types of matter based on how their constituent particles interact with one another.

steps.title: Chemical Bonding vs Physical Combination
steps.content:
The fundamental difference lies in the type of force holding the components together:

*   **Compounds:** The elements are joined together through **chemical bonds** (such as ionic or covalent bonds). This creates a new substance with entirely different properties from the original elements.
*   **Mixtures:** The components are simply **physically intermingled** or combined. They are not chemically bonded, meaning they retain their individual chemical properties and can usually be separated by physical means (like filtration or evaporation).

steps.title: State the final difference
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme requirements, the most direct way to state the difference is:

**A compound has components that are chemically bonded, whereas a mixture has components that are physically combined.**

highlights.text: State one difference between a compound and a mixture.

steps.title: Identify the core concept
steps.content: To distinguish between a compound and a mixture, we look at how the components are put together. This falls under **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds, and Mixtures**. The most reliable way to tell them apart is by looking at whether their recipe (composition) is strictly set or if it can change.

steps.title: Analyze compound composition
steps.content:
A compound consists of different elements that are **chemically bonded** together. Because these bonds form in specific ways, a compound always has a **fixed (definite) composition**. 

For example, pure carbon dioxide ($CO_{2}$) will always contain exactly one carbon atom for every two oxygen atoms. You cannot change this ratio without changing the substance itself.

steps.title: Analyze mixture composition
steps.content:
A mixture consists of different substances that are merely **physically combined** without chemical bonds. Because there are no bonds holding them in a specific arrangement, a mixture has a **variable composition**. 

For example, you can have a salt-water mixture that is very salty (lots of salt) or lightly salty (very little salt). Both are still mixtures because you can vary the proportions of the components.

steps.title: State the final difference
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the fundamental difference is how they are joined, which dictates their ratio. A compound has a fixed ratio of elements due to chemical bonding, while a mixture has variable proportions because the parts are only physically combined.

**Accepted Answer:** A compound has a fixed composition, whereas a mixture has a variable composition.

highlights.text: A compound has components that are chemically bonded; a mixture has components that are physically combined.

steps.title: Analyze the nature of substances
steps.content: To answer this question, we first need to understand how the components of a substance are held together. In **Topic S1.1.1 Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**, we learn that the fundamental difference lies in the type of connection between the particles.

highlights.text: State one difference between a compound and a mixture.

steps.title: Compare separation methods
steps.content:
Using the **Method of Separation**, we can distinguish them by how they are broken down:

*   **Mixtures**: The components are not chemically bonded, so they can be separated using **physical processes** such as filtration, distillation, or chromatography.
*   **Compounds**: The elements are chemically bonded in fixed ratios, meaning they can only be separated into their constituent elements through **chemical means**, such as electrolysis.

steps.title: State the final difference
steps.content:
The most direct way to state this difference for your exam is to focus on the bonding. 

A compound has components that are **chemically bonded**, whereas a mixture has components that are simply **physically combined**.

steps.title: Classify the combination
steps.content:
To distinguish between a compound and a mixture, we look at how the substances are combined. This concept is found in **Topic S1.1.1: Elements, Compounds and Mixtures**. 

In a **mixture**, substances are simply mixed together without any chemical reaction taking place. In a **compound**, the elements are joined by chemical bonds to form a new substance.

steps.title: Analyze property retention
steps.content:
The most visible difference lies in their properties:

*   **Mixture:** The components are **physically combined**, so they **retain their individual properties**. For example, in a mixture of sand and salt, the salt still tastes salty and the sand is still gritty.
*   **Compound:** The components are **chemically bonded**, so the compound has **properties distinct** from its constituent elements. For example, sodium (a reactive metal) and chlorine (a poisonous gas) chemically bond to form table salt ($NaCl$), which has completely different properties from either element.

highlights.text: compound has components that are chemically bonded
mixture has components that are physically combined

steps.title: State the final difference
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme, the core difference is the nature of how the components are held together. 

A compound consists of elements that are chemically bonded in a fixed ratio, whereas a mixture consists of substances that are physically combined in any ratio.

parts.content: Outline how filtration and distillation can be used to separate components of a mixture of sand, salt and water.
parts.markscheme:
- Filtration separates sand from the saltwater by trapping solid sand on filter paper. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Distillation separates salt from water by evaporating the water, then condensing it. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the mixture properties
steps.content: To separate this mixture, we first identify the physical properties of the components. Sand is an **insoluble solid**, while salt is **soluble** in water. Because the sand does not dissolve, we can use a two-step sequential process starting with a technique that separates solids from liquids.

steps.title: Filtration to separate sand
steps.content:
The first step is **filtration**. You pour the mixture through a funnel lined with filter paper. 

*   The solid sand is too large to pass through the paper and is trapped as the **residue**.
*   The saltwater solution passes through the paper and is collected as the **filtrate**.

This step earns the first mark by effectively removing the insoluble component from the liquid phase.

highlights.text: Filtration separates sand from the saltwater by trapping solid sand on filter paper.

steps.title: Distillation to recover salt
steps.content:
The second step is **distillation** to separate the dissolved salt from the water. You heat the saltwater filtrate in a distillation flask:

1.  **Evaporation:** The water boils and turns into steam (vapor), leaving the solid salt behind in the flask.
2.  **Condensation:** The water vapor travels through a condenser (cooled by water), where it cools down and turns back into liquid water to be collected separately.

This process allows you to recover both the pure salt and the pure water, earning the second mark.

highlights.text: Distillation separates salt from water by evaporating the water, then condensing it.

parts.content:
A student is given an unknown substance. The student performs tests and observes the following:
- It melts sharply at $801\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$.
- It does not conduct electricity in the solid state but conducts when dissolved in water.
- It forms colourless crystals.

Deduce whether the substance is most likely an element, compound or mixture, giving a reason for your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- Compound <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Because it has a sharp melting point and conducts electricity when dissolved (indicating ionic bonding). <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Analyze the melting point
steps.content:
The first clue is that the substance **melts sharply** at a specific temperature ($801\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$). In chemistry, a sharp melting point is a characteristic of a **pure substance**. 

Mixtures usually melt over a range of temperatures because the different components have different melting points. Since this substance has a fixed, sharp melting point, we can deduce it is either an **element** or a **compound**, but not a mixture.

steps.title: Evaluate conductivity and bonding
steps.content:
Next, we look at the electrical properties. The substance is an insulator as a solid but **conducts when dissolved in water**. 

As explained in **Topic S2.1.3 (Lattice structures and properties)**, this behavior is the classic signature of an **ionic substance**. 
- In the solid state, the ions are locked in a fixed lattice and cannot move to carry a charge.
- When dissolved (or molten), the lattice breaks down, and the ions are free to move, allowing the liquid to conduct electricity.

steps.title: Deduce the classification
steps.content:
By combining these observations, we can reach a conclusion:
1. It is a pure substance (sharp melting point).
2. It has ionic bonding (specific conductivity behavior).

Since ionic bonds form between different types of atoms (typically a metal and a non-metal) to form a new substance, it must be a **compound**. For example, Sodium Chloride ($NaCl$) also melts at $801\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and shows these exact properties.

steps.title: Final answer and marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must state the classification and provide the reasoning based on the data provided.

**Classification:** Compound
**Reason:** It has a sharp melting point (indicating it is pure) and conducts electricity when dissolved (indicating it is ionic).

steps.title: Analyze the melting point
steps.content:
The first piece of evidence is that the substance **melts sharply** at $801\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$. In chemistry, a "sharp" melting point (melting at one specific temperature) is a characteristic of a **pure substance**. 

Mixtures do not have a single melting point; instead, they melt over a range of temperatures because the different components within the mixture melt at different points. Therefore, we can eliminate the possibility that this substance is a mixture.

steps.title: Evaluate conductivity for elements
steps.content:
Next, we look at the electrical conductivity. The substance does not conduct as a solid but does conduct when dissolved in water. 

If the substance were an **element**, it would likely be either a metal or a non-metal:
- **Metals** conduct electricity in the solid state due to delocalized electrons.
- **Non-metals** (like sulfur or phosphorus) generally do not conduct electricity at all, whether solid or in solution.

Since this substance is non-conductive as a solid, it cannot be a metal. Since it *does* conduct when dissolved, it does not fit the typical behavior of a simple non-metallic element. We can therefore eliminate "element" as the most likely choice.

highlights.text: It does not conduct electricity in the solid state but conducts when dissolved in water.

steps.title: Identify as a compound
steps.content:
By process of elimination, the substance must be a **compound**. Specifically, the properties described are classic indicators of an **ionic compound**:
1. **High, sharp melting point:** Results from a strong ionic lattice structure.
2. **Conductivity behavior:** In the solid state, ions are locked in position and cannot carry charge. When dissolved (or molten), the lattice breaks down, and the ions are free to move and conduct electricity.

This behavior is covered in **Topic S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Final Deduction
steps.content:
Based on the evidence:
- The sharp melting point rules out a mixture.
- The conductivity behavior (solid vs. dissolved) rules out elements and identifies it as an ionic compound.

**Final Answer:** Compound. 
**Reason:** It has a sharp melting point (indicating it is pure) and conducts electricity only when dissolved, which is characteristic of ionic bonding.

parts.content: Explain why evaporation and crystallization are not suitable methods to separate the components of a homogeneous mixture of ethanol and water.
parts.markscheme:
- Evaporation and crystallization are used to separate solid solutes from liquid solvents. <marks> 1 mark </marks>
- Both ethanol and water are volatile liquids, so no solid crystals would form. <marks> 1 mark </marks>

steps.title: Define the purpose of techniques
steps.content: To understand why these methods are unsuitable, we first look at what they are designed to do. **Evaporation** and **crystallization** are separation techniques specifically used to recover a **solid solute** from a **liquid solvent** (such as separating salt from water). These methods rely on the fact that the solid does not vaporize, while the liquid does.

highlights.text: Evaporation and crystallization are used to separate solid solutes from liquid solvents.

steps.title: Analyze component properties
steps.content: Next, we examine the physical properties of the mixture components. Ethanol and water are both **volatile liquids**. This means they both have a tendency to vaporize when heated. Crucially, neither component is a solid, nor will they naturally form a solid lattice (crystals) under the conditions of simple evaporation.

highlights.text: Both ethanol and water are volatile liquids

steps.title: Identify the conceptual mismatch
steps.content: Because both substances are volatile liquids, if you were to heat the mixture, both the ethanol and the water would evaporate into the air. Since there is no non-volatile solid present in the homogeneous mixture, **no solid crystals would form** in the evaporating dish. You would simply lose both components to the atmosphere (or the collection flask) without achieving a separation into solid form.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In summary, evaporation and crystallization fail because they require a **phase difference** (solid vs. liquid) that does not exist in an ethanol-water mixture. 

To earn full marks on this question, you must mention:
1. These methods are intended for **solid-liquid** separation.
2. Both ethanol and water are **volatile liquids**, preventing crystal formation.

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To calculate the amount of substance in moles ($n$), we use the relationship between mass ($m$) and molar mass ($M$). This concept is found in **Topic S1.4: The mole**.

From the Data Booklet, we use:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

steps.title: Calculate moles of Helium
steps.content:
Substitute the given values for Helium ($m = 2.00\text{ g}$, $M = 4.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$):

$$\text{n(He)} = \frac{2.00}{4.00} = 0.500\text{ mol}$$

- $\text{N}_2: 0.5000\text{ mol}$

- $\text{CO}_2: 0.5000\text{ mol}$

steps.title: Understand Dimensional Analysis
steps.content:
To calculate the number of moles using **dimensional analysis**, we treat the molar mass as a conversion factor. Molar mass ($M$) tells us the mass of $1$ mole of a substance. By setting up a ratio where the unit 'grams' cancels out, we can solve for moles ($n$). This is based on the fundamental relationship found in the data booklet: 

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

steps.title: Calculate Moles of Helium
steps.content:
For Helium (He), the sample mass is $2.00\text{ g}$ and the molar mass is $4.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. We multiply the mass by the conversion factor (moles per gram):

$$2.00\text{ g He} \times \left( \frac{1\text{ mol He}}{4.00\text{ g He}} \right) = 0.500\text{ mol He}$$

*Note: We keep three significant figures to match the given data.*

steps.title: Understand Proportional Reasoning
steps.content:
To find the number of moles using proportional reasoning, we compare the mass of the sample we have to the mass of exactly one mole (the molar mass). 

If 1 mole of a substance has a mass of $M$ grams, then a sample of $m$ grams represents a specific fraction or multiple of a mole. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**.

steps.title: Identify the mole formula
steps.content:
In your IB Data Booklet, under the **Key Chemistry Equations** section, you will find the formula that represents this proportional relationship:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Where:
- $n$ is the amount in moles (mol)
- $m$ is the mass of the sample (g)
- $M$ is the molar mass ($\text{g mol}^{-1}$)

steps.title: Calculate Helium (He) moles
steps.content:
For Helium, we compare the sample mass of $2.00\text{ g}$ to its molar mass of $4.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

Since $2.00$ is exactly half of $4.00$, the sample is $0.5$ moles. To maintain significant figures (3 sig figs from the data provided):

$$\text{He}: n = \frac{2.00}{4.00} = 0.500\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate Nitrogen (N2) moles
steps.content:
For Nitrogen, we compare $14.01\text{ g}$ to $28.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

Similarly, this is exactly half of the molar mass. Using 4 significant figures as provided in the table:

$$\text{N}_2: n = \frac{14.01}{28.02} = 0.5000\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate Oxygen (O2) moles
steps.content:
For Oxygen, we compare $8.00\text{ g}$ to $32.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

Here, $8$ is one-quarter of $32$. Calculating the ratio:

$$\text{O}_2: n = \frac{8.00}{32.00} = 0.250\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Calculate CO2 moles
steps.content:
Finally, for Carbon Dioxide, we compare $22.00\text{ g}$ to $44.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}$. 

Again, the sample mass is exactly half of the molar mass:

$$\text{CO}_2: n = \frac{22.00}{44.00} = 0.5000\text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Final Answers
steps.content:
The calculated amounts are:
- $\text{He}: 0.500\text{ mol}$
- $\text{N}_2: 0.5000\text{ mol}$
- $\text{O}_2: 0.250\text{ mol}$

- **State** the correct final answers with appropriate significant figures: <marks>A1</marks>
  $\text{He}: 0.500 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.01 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
  $\text{N}_2: 0.5000 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
  $\text{O}_2: 0.250 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.51 \times 10^{23}$ molecules

steps.title: Identify required formulas
steps.content:
To find the number of molecules, we first need to determine the amount of substance in moles ($n$). We can use the formula from the data booklet relating mass ($m$) and molar mass ($M$). Then, we multiply the number of moles by the Avogadro constant ($N_A$) to find the number of particles ($N$).

$$\text{moles } (n) = \frac{\text{mass } (m)}{\text{molar mass } (M)}$$

$$\text{number of particles } (N) = n \times N_A$$

steps.title: Calculate moles for each gas
steps.content:
Using the data provided in the table, calculate the number of moles for each sample. Be sure to track the significant figures from the input data (the mass and molar mass).

$$\begin{aligned} n(\text{He}) &= \frac{2.00 \text{ g}}{4.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.500 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{N}_2) &= \frac{14.01 \text{ g}}{28.02 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.5000 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{O}_2) &= \frac{8.00 \text{ g}}{32.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.250 \text{ mol} \\ n(\text{CO}_2) &= \frac{22.00 \text{ g}}{44.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.5000 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the mass values by the molar mass values.

desmos.instructions: Perform each division to find the number of moles.

calculator.description: Calculate moles for each sample.

steps.title: Calculate total molecules
steps.content:
Now, multiply the number of moles by Avogadro's number ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$) to find the number of molecules for each. Round your answers to the correct number of significant figures based on the initial data.

$$\begin{aligned} N(\text{He}) &= 0.500 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.01 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms} \\ N(\text{N}_2) &= 0.5000 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules} \\ N(\text{O}_2) &= 0.250 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.51 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules} \\ N(\text{CO}_2) &= 0.5000 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
The number of molecules (or atoms for Helium) in each sample is:

- **Helium:** $3.01 \times 10^{23}$ 
- **Nitrogen:** $3.011 \times 10^{23}$
- **Oxygen:** $1.51 \times 10^{23}$
- **Carbon Dioxide:** $3.011 \times 10^{23}$

This calculation follows **Structure 1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole** in your syllabus. You earn 1 mark for the correct mole to molecule calculation method ($M1$) and 1 mark for the correct final values with appropriate significant figures ($A1$).

steps.title: Use Dimensional Analysis
steps.content:
To find the number of particles (atoms or molecules) in a single step, we use **dimensional analysis**. This involves multiplying the mass of the sample by the reciprocal of the molar mass to get moles, and then by the Avogadro constant to get the number of particles.

The conversion string looks like this:
$$\text{Number of particles} = \text{mass (g)} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{\text{Molar Mass (g)}} \times \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ particles}}{1 \text{ mol}}$$

This uses concepts from **Topic S1.4: Counting particles by mass: The mole**.

steps.title: Calculate Helium particles
steps.content:
Using the data for Helium (He):
- Mass = $2.00 \text{ g}$
- Molar Mass = $4.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Atoms of He} = 2.00 \text{ g} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{4.00 \text{ g}} \times (6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms mol}^{-1})$$

$$\text{Atoms of He} = 0.500 \text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures (based on $2.00 \text{ g}$), we get **$3.01 \times 10^{23}$ atoms**.

steps.title: Calculate Nitrogen molecules
steps.content:
Using the data for Nitrogen ($N_2$):
- Mass = $14.01 \text{ g}$
- Molar Mass = $28.02 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Molecules of } N_2 = 14.01 \text{ g} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{28.02 \text{ g}} \times (6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules mol}^{-1})$$

$$\text{Molecules of } N_2 = 0.5000 \text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23}$$

Based on the 4 significant figures in $14.01 \text{ g}$, the final answer is **$3.011 \times 10^{23}$ molecules**.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of Nitrogen molecules.

steps.title: Calculate Oxygen molecules
steps.content:
Using the data for Oxygen ($O_2$):
- Mass = $8.00 \text{ g}$
- Molar Mass = $32.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Molecules of } O_2 = 8.00 \text{ g} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{32.00 \text{ g}} \times (6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules mol}^{-1})$$

$$\text{Molecules of } O_2 = 0.250 \text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.5055 \times 10^{23}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures (based on $8.00 \text{ g}$), we get **$1.51 \times 10^{23}$ molecules**.

steps.title: Calculate Carbon Dioxide molecules
steps.content:
Using the data for Carbon Dioxide ($CO_2$):
- Mass = $22.00 \text{ g}$
- Molar Mass = $44.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$

$$\text{Molecules of } CO_2 = 22.00 \text{ g} \times \frac{1 \text{ mol}}{44.00 \text{ g}} \times (6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules mol}^{-1})$$

$$\text{Molecules of } CO_2 = 0.5000 \text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23}$$

Based on the 4 significant figures in $22.00 \text{ g}$, the final answer is **$3.011 \times 10^{23}$ molecules**.

steps.title: Identify proportional relationship
steps.content:
To find the number of molecules, we first determine the ratio between the mass of the sample and its molar mass. This ratio tells us what fraction of a mole we have. 

We will use the **proportional reasoning** approach: once we find this decimal ratio, we simply multiply it by Avogadro's constant ($6.022 \times 10^{23}$) to find the total number of particles.

steps.title: Calculate the amount in moles
steps.content:
To compare the number of particles, we first need to determine the amount of substance in moles ($n$) for each gas. We use the formula from the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Applying this to each sample:
- **Helium (He):** $n = \frac{2.00\text{ g}}{4.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.500\text{ mol}$
- **Nitrogen (N$_2$):** $n = \frac{14.01\text{ g}}{28.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.500\text{ mol}$
- **Oxygen (O$_2$):** $n = \frac{8.00\text{ g}}{32.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.250\text{ mol}$
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO$_2$):** $n = \frac{22.00\text{ g}}{44.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.500\text{ mol}$

steps.title: Calculate absolute number of molecules
steps.content:
Next, we multiply the number of moles by the Avogadro constant ($N_A$) to find the absolute number of molecules in each sample. 

$$\text{Number of particles} = n \times N_A$$

Using the value $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ as specified:
- **He, N$_2$, and CO$_2$:**
  $$0.500\text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23}\text{ molecules}$$
- **Oxygen (O$_2$):**
  $$0.250\text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.506 \times 10^{23}\text{ molecules}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 0.5 * 6.022 * 10^23 and 0.25 * 6.022 * 10^23 into the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Type 0.5 * 6.022E23 then 0.25 * 6.022E23. Use the [2nd] [,] keys for the 'E' (scientific notation) function.

steps.title: Compare the results
steps.content:
Now we compare our calculated values to identify the trend:
- The samples of **Helium**, **Nitrogen**, and **Carbon Dioxide** all contain the same number of molecules ($3.01 \times 10^{23}$).
- The **Oxygen** sample contains exactly half that amount ($1.51 \times 10^{23}$ molecules).

steps.title: Relate to particulate nature
steps.content:
The trend observed demonstrates a fundamental principle of the particulate nature of matter: 

1. **Equal amounts in moles contain equal numbers of particles**, regardless of the identity or mass of the gas. This is why He, N$_2$, and CO$_2$ (all 0.500 mol) have the same molecule count.
2. **Particle count depends on the amount of substance (moles)**, not the physical mass. 

Even though the mass of the Nitrogen sample (14.01 g) is much higher than the Helium sample (2.00 g), they contain the same number of particles because they contain the same number of moles.

steps.title: Calculate amount in moles
steps.content:
To compare the number of particles, we first need to determine the amount in moles ($n$) for each gas sample. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Applying this to our data:
- **Helium (He):** $n = \frac{2.00\text{ g}}{4.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.50\text{ mol}$
- **Nitrogen ($\text{N}_2$):** $n = \frac{14.01\text{ g}}{28.02\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.50\text{ mol}$
- **Oxygen ($\text{O}_2$):** $n = \frac{8.00\text{ g}}{32.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.25\text{ mol}$
- **Carbon Dioxide ($\text{CO}_2$):** $n = \frac{22.00\text{ g}}{44.00\text{ g mol}^{-1}} = 0.50\text{ mol}$

steps.title: Calculate number of molecules
steps.content:
Using Avogadro's constant ($N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$), we can find the exact number of particles ($N$) using $N = n \times N_A$:

- **For samples with 0.50 mol (He, $\text{N}_2$, $\text{CO}_2$):**
$$0.50 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}$$

- **For the sample with 0.25 mol ($\text{O}_2$):**
$$0.25 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 1.506 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules}$$

Helium, Nitrogen, and Carbon Dioxide have the same number of molecules, while Oxygen has half as many.

steps.title: Explain the molar trend
steps.content:
The observed trend is explained by the fundamental principle of the mole: **equal amounts in moles contain equal numbers of particles**, regardless of the chemical identity or mass of the substance. 

Even though 2.00 g of He and 22.00 g of $\text{CO}_2$ have very different masses, they represent the same fraction of a mole (0.50 mol). This reflects the particulate nature of matter: we count matter by the number of units (atoms or molecules) rather than just its total weight.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
In conclusion:
1.  **Helium, Nitrogen, and Carbon Dioxide** samples each contain $3.01 \times 10^{23}$ molecules because they all consist of **0.500 mol**.
2.  **Oxygen** contains fewer molecules ($1.51 \times 10^{23}$) because it is a smaller amount (**0.250 mol**).
3.  The number of particles depends strictly on the **amount of substance (moles)**, not the mass of the sample.

parts.content: Explain the difference between an atom, an ion, and a molecule.
parts.markscheme:
Atom: The smallest unit of an element that retains the chemical properties of that element, consisting of protons, neutrons, and electrons <marks>A1</marks>

Ion: An atom or molecule that has gained or lost one or more electrons, resulting in a net positive or negative charge (e.g. $\mathrm{Na}^+$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^-$) <marks>A1</marks>

Molecule: A group of two or more atoms covalently bonded together, forming a neutral particle with a defined structure (e.g. $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is a molecule made up of one carbon atom and two oxygen atoms) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define the Atom
steps.content:
To understand the particulate nature of matter, we start with the **atom**. This is the fundamental building block. It is defined as the smallest unit of an element that retains the chemical properties of that element.

Atoms are composed of three subatomic particles: **protons**, **neutrons**, and **electrons**. In its ground state, an atom is electrically neutral because the number of positive protons in the nucleus exactly balances the number of negative electrons surrounding it. This concept is covered in **Structure 1.2: The nuclear atom** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: The smallest unit of an element that retains the chemical properties of that element, consisting of protons, neutrons, and electrons

steps.title: Define the Ion
steps.content:
An **ion** is a species that carries a net electrical charge. Unlike a neutral atom, an ion is formed when an atom or molecule has gained or lost one or more electrons.

- If an atom **loses** electrons, it becomes a positively charged **cation** (e.g., $\mathrm{Na}^+$).
- If an atom **gains** electrons, it becomes a negatively charged **anion** (e.g., $\mathrm{Cl}^-$).

You can find more on this in **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**.

highlights.text: An atom or molecule that has gained or lost one or more electrons, resulting in a net positive or negative charge (e.g. $\mathrm{Na}^+$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^-$)

steps.title: Define the Molecule
steps.content:
A **molecule** consists of a group of two or more atoms that are chemically bonded together. Specifically, these atoms are held together by **covalent bonds**, which involve the sharing of electron pairs.

A molecule acts as a single, neutral particle with a specific structure. For example, a water molecule ($\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$) is made of two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. This is explored further in **Structure 2.2: The covalent model**.

highlights.text: A group of two or more atoms covalently bonded together, forming a neutral particle with a defined structure (e.g. $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is a molecule made up of one carbon atom and two oxygen atoms)

steps.title: Summary Comparison
steps.content:
In summary, the key differences are based on charge and composition:

- **Atom**: Neutral, single unit of an element.
- **Ion**: Charged, formed by electron transfer.
- **Molecule**: Neutral, group of atoms covalently bonded.

Each of these descriptions corresponds to 1 mark in the markscheme, totaling 3 marks for providing all three definitions correctly.

steps.title: Categorizing Particulate Matter
steps.content: To distinguish between these three particles, we need to look at the balance of subatomic particles (protons and electrons) and how they are organized. This relates directly to **Topic S1.2: The nuclear atom** and **Topic S2.2: The covalent model**.

highlights.text: Explain the difference between an atom, an ion, and a molecule.

steps.title: Atoms: Neutral Subatomic Balance
steps.content:
An **atom** is the smallest unit of an element. In its ground state, the ratio of protons ($p^+$) to electrons ($e^-$) is exactly $1:1$. 

Because the number of positive charges equals the number of negative charges, the net charge is zero:
$$\text{Net Charge} = (\text{number of } p^+) - (\text{number of } e^-) = 0$$

According to the markscheme, you should define it as the smallest unit of an element that retains its chemical properties.

steps.title: Ions: Imbalanced Electrical Charge
steps.content:
An **ion** is an atom (or group of atoms) that has lost or gained electrons, breaking the $1:1$ ratio. This creates a net electrical charge.

*   **Cation (Positive):** More protons than electrons ($p^+ > e^-$). For example, $\mathrm{Na}^+$ has 11 protons and 10 electrons.
*   **Anion (Negative):** More electrons than protons ($e^- > p^+$). For example, $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ has 17 protons and 18 electrons.

In the exam, specify that it is an atom or molecule that has gained or lost one or more electrons.

steps.title: Molecules: Sharing Electron Density
steps.content:
A **molecule** is a structure where two or more atoms share electrons. Unlike individual atoms where electrons are attracted to a single nucleus, in a molecule, valence electrons are shared between multiple nuclei via **covalent bonds**.

Key characteristics:
*   It is a neutral particle overall (the sum of all $p^+$ equals the sum of all $e^-$).
*   It has a defined chemical structure (e.g., $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ or $\mathrm{CO}_2$).

You earn the final mark by mentioning that it is a group of atoms covalently bonded together.

steps.title: Find Avogadro's constant
steps.content:
Next, we look at the **Physical Constants** section of the IB Data Booklet to find the **Avogadro constant** ($N_{\text{A}}$). This constant tells us the number of particles in exactly one mole of any substance.

$$N_{\text{A}} = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Apply the particle formula
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Formula Approach**, we can find the total number of particles ($N$) by multiplying the number of moles ($n$) by Avogadro's constant ($N_{\text{A}}$):

$$\begin{aligned} N &= n \times N_{\text{A}} \\ N &= 0.250 \text{ mol} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation helps us convert the macroscopic measurement (moles) into a microscopic count of molecules.

gdc.instructions: Enter the multiplication using scientific notation.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication directly.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into the scientific calculator.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of particles.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the product gives us $1.505 \times 10^{23}$. 

In IB Chemistry, it is crucial to round your final answer to the correct number of significant figures. Since our input ($0.250$ mol) has three significant figures, we round our result accordingly.

$$\text{Number of molecules} = 1.51 \times 10^{23}$$

Always remember to include the units or the type of particle (molecules of $\mathrm{CO}_2$) to gain full marks!

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, let's identify what the question provides and what we need to find. We are given the amount of substance in moles and asked to find the total number of particles (molecules) of $\mathrm{CO}_2$.

- **Given**: $0.250 \text{ mol}$
- **Goal**: Number of particles

steps.title: Identify Avogadro's constant
steps.content:
In IB Chemistry, the bridge between "moles" and "particles" is the Avogadro constant ($N_{\text{A}}$). You can find this value in the **Physical Constants** section of your data booklet.

$$N_{\text{A}} = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Apply dimensional analysis
steps.content:
Using the dimensional analysis approach, we treat units like algebraic variables. We set up the calculation so that the unit "mol" cancels out, leaving us with "particles".

$$\text{Number of particles} = 0.250 \text{ mol} \times \frac{6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ particles}}{1 \text{ mol}}$$

steps.title: Final calculation and rounding
steps.content:
Now, perform the calculation. Following the exam tip, we must provide the final answer to **3 significant figures**.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Number of particles} &= 1.505 \times 10^{23} \\ &\approx 1.51 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules} \end{aligned}$$

This earns both marks: one for the correct setup and one for the accurate final answer.

gdc.instructions: Input 0.250 multiplied by 6.02 times 10 raised to the power of 23.

ti84.instructions: Type 0.250, press ×, type 6.02, press 2nd then , (EE), type 23, and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of particles by multiplying moles by Avogadro's constant.

highlights.text: Final answer must be given to 3 significant figures

steps.title: Identify the mole relationship
steps.content:
To solve this using proportional reasoning, we first need to identify the relationship between the number of moles and the number of particles. This relationship is defined by the **Avogadro constant**, which tells us how many particles are in exactly one mole of a substance.

From the Data Booklet, we see:
$$
N_{\text{A}} = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}
$$

This means that $1.00 \text{ mol}$ is equivalent to $6.02 \times 10^{23}$ particles (in this case, molecules of $\mathrm{CO}_2$).

steps.title: Set up the proportion
steps.content:
Now, we set up a ratio comparing the known molar relationship to our specific problem. Let $x$ represent the unknown number of particles in $0.250 \text{ mol}$:

$$\frac{1.00 \text{ mol}}{6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ particles}} = \frac{0.250 \text{ mol}}{x \text{ particles}}$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
We can solve for $x$ by cross-multiplying the terms of the proportion:

$$\begin{aligned} 1.00 \cdot x &= 0.250 \cdot (6.02 \times 10^{23}) \\ x &= 0.250 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find the numerical value.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.250, then press [×], then enter 6.02, press [2nd] [EE] (to get the 'E' for scientific notation), enter 23, and press [ENTER].

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of particles.

steps.title: Apply significant figures
steps.content:
The raw calculation gives $1.505 \times 10^{23}$. 

However, in IB Chemistry, we must round our final answer to the appropriate number of significant figures. Since the value $0.250$ has 3 significant figures, our final answer should also have 3 significant figures.

$$x \approx 1.51 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules of } \mathrm{CO}_2$$

parts.content:
A sample of gas contains 5.00 g of oxygen gas $\mathrm{O}_2$.

Calculate the number of moles of oxygen gas in this sample.
parts.markscheme:
Molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_2 = 2 \times 16.00 = 32.00 \text{ g/mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

$\text{Moles of } \mathrm{O}_2 = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{molar mass}} = \frac{5.00 \text{ g}}{32.00 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.156 \text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: To start, let's identify what we know from the question. We are given the mass of the oxygen gas and its chemical formula.

steps.title: Calculate molar mass
steps.content:
Oxygen gas is diatomic, meaning it exists as $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecules. Using the relative atomic mass of oxygen ($16.00$) from the periodic table, we calculate the molar mass ($M$) of $\mathrm{O}_2$:

$$M(\mathrm{O}_2) = 2 \times 16.00 = 32.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

This step earns the first mark.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 16.00 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * 16.00 into a new expression line.

calculator.description: Calculate the molar mass of diatomic oxygen.

steps.title: Apply the mole formula
steps.content:
Now we use the standard mole equation found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet to find the number of moles ($n$).

$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

Substituting our values:

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{5.00 \text{ g}}{32.00 \text{ g mol}^{-1}} \\ n &= 0.15625 \text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Divide the mass by the molar mass to find moles.

steps.title: Final answer and rounding
steps.content:
Finally, we round our answer to the appropriate number of significant figures. Since the given mass ($5.00 \text{ g}$) has three significant figures, our final answer should also be given to three significant figures.

$$\text{Moles of } \mathrm{O}_2 = 0.156 \text{ mol}$$

steps.title: Determine the molar mass
steps.content:
To convert grams to moles, we first need the molar mass ($M$) of oxygen gas. Oxygen gas is diatomic, meaning it exists as $\mathrm{O}_2$. 

Looking at the periodic table, the relative atomic mass of one oxygen atom is $16.00$. Therefore, the molar mass of $\mathrm{O}_2$ is:

$$M(\mathrm{O}_2) = 2 \times 16.00 = 32.00 \text{ g/mol}$$

This value tells us that $1 \text{ mole of } \mathrm{O}_2$ has a mass of $32.00 \text{ grams}$. We will use this as our conversion factor.

steps.title: Set up dimensional analysis
steps.content:
In dimensional analysis, we start with the given value and multiply it by a conversion factor (a fraction) so that the unwanted units cancel out and we are left with the desired unit.

We start with $5.00 \text{ g}$ of $\mathrm{O}_2$ and want to find moles. To cancel the "grams" unit, we place $32.00 \text{ g}$ in the denominator:

$$\text{moles of } \mathrm{O}_2 = 5.00 \text{ g } \mathrm{O}_2 \times \left( \frac{1 \text{ mol } \mathrm{O}_2}{32.00 \text{ g } \mathrm{O}_2} \right)$$

Notice how the units of "g $\mathrm{O}_2$" cancel out, leaving us with "mol $\mathrm{O}_2$". This is essentially a single-step setup of the formula found in your data booklet:

$$\text{n} = \frac{\text{m}}{\text{M}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final answer
steps.content:
Now, perform the division. 

$$\frac{5.00}{32.00} = 0.15625 \text{ mol}$$

According to the IB rounding rules for significant figures, since our initial mass ($5.00 \text{ g}$) has **three significant figures**, our final answer should also be expressed to three significant figures.

$$\text{moles of } \mathrm{O}_2 = 0.156 \text{ mol}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation screen, input 5.00 / 32.00.

ti84.instructions: Enter 5.00 divided by 32.00 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles by dividing mass by molar mass.

parts.content: Calculate the total number of oxygen atoms in the sample.
parts.markscheme:
Number of oxygen atoms = moles $\times$ Avogadro's number $\times$ atoms per molecule <marks>M1</marks>

$\text{Total oxygen atoms} = 0.156 \text{ mol} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1} \times 2$ <marks>M1</marks>

$= 1.88 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}$ <marks>A1</marks>

<examTip>Remember to include the factor of 2 since each $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule contains 2 oxygen atoms</examTip>

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content: To solve this using the **Molecules to Atoms Approach**, we first identify our starting point: we have $0.156$ moles of oxygen gas ($O_2$). We also need the **Avogadro constant ($N_A$)** from the Data Booklet to help us convert from moles to a count of particles.

steps.title: Calculate total molecules
steps.content:
First, we calculate the total number of oxygen molecules ($O_2$) by multiplying the number of moles by Avogadro's constant:

$$\text{Number of molecules} = n \times N_A$$
$$\text{Number of molecules} = 0.156 \text{ mol} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\text{Number of molecules} = 9.39 \times 10^{22} \text{ molecules}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.156 * 6.02E23 (using the 'EE' key for the exponent).

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of molecules.

steps.title: Relate molecules to atoms
steps.content:
Next, we look at the chemical formula of oxygen gas, which is $O_2$. This formula tells us that **one molecule** of oxygen contains **two atoms** of oxygen. 

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{2 \text{ O atoms}}{1 \text{ O}_2 \text{ molecule}}$$

highlights.text: Remember to include the factor of 2 since each $\mathrm{O}_2$ molecule contains 2 oxygen atoms

steps.title: Calculate total atoms
steps.content:
Finally, we multiply the total number of molecules we found in Step 1 by the 2 oxygen atoms per molecule:

$$\text{Total atoms} = 9.39 \times 10^{22} \text{ molecules} \times 2 \frac{\text{atoms}}{\text{molecule}}$$
$$\text{Total atoms} = 1.88 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}$$

This gives us our final answer, rounded to three significant figures to match the precision of the initial measurement ($0.156$).

ti84.instructions: Multiply the previous result by 2.

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of atoms by multiplying the molecules by 2.

steps.title: Identify atoms per molecule
steps.content:
To use the **Moles of Atoms Approach**, we first need to look at the chemical formula of oxygen gas, which is $\text{O}_2$. 

Each molecule of oxygen gas contains exactly **2 oxygen atoms**. This is a crucial first step because we aren't just counting molecules; we are counting the individual atoms within those molecules.

highlights.text: Remember to include the factor of 2 since each \mathrm{O}_2$ molecule contains 2 oxygen atoms

steps.title: Calculate moles of oxygen atoms
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the total number of moles of oxygen atoms. We do this by multiplying the number of moles of the sample ($0.156 \text{ mol}$) by the number of atoms per molecule ($2$).

$$\text{moles of O atoms} = 0.156 \text{ mol} \times 2 = 0.312 \text{ mol}$$

This tells us that in our sample, there are $0.312$ moles of individual oxygen atoms.

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.156 * 2 and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Verify the multiplication of sample moles by the number of atoms.

steps.title: Calculate total number of atoms
steps.content:
Finally, to find the actual number of particles (atoms), we use the relationship between moles ($n$) and the Avogadro constant ($N_A$) found in your **Data Booklet**.

$$\text{Total atoms} = n_{\text{atoms}} \times N_A$$

Substituting our values:
$$\text{Total oxygen atoms} = 0.312 \text{ mol} \times 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\text{Total oxygen atoms} = 1.88 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms}$$

(Note: The markscheme uses $6.022$, but the IB Data Booklet typically provides $6.02$; both lead to the same rounded answer of $1.88 \times 10^{23}$ atoms for 3 significant figures.)

ti84.instructions: Enter 0.312 * 6.02E23 and press ENTER. (Use the 'EE' button for the exponent).

calculator.description: Calculate the final number of atoms by multiplying the total moles of atoms by Avogadro's constant.

parts.content: Explain the changes in particle arrangement and energy that occur when a solid is heated until it becomes a gas.
parts.markscheme:
- Particles gain kinetic energy and vibrate more vigorously as solid is heated [1]
- Particles overcome forces holding them in fixed positions, causing melting [1]
- Further heating causes liquid particles to gain more kinetic energy [1]
- Particles overcome intermolecular forces completely, becoming a gas through evaporation/boiling [1]

<note>Award marks for clear progression from solid → liquid → gas with correct reference to energy and forces at each stage</note>

steps.title: Solid state and initial heating
steps.content:
In a solid, particles are arranged in a regular lattice and are held in fixed positions by strong attractive forces. As we begin heating the solid, the particles gain **kinetic energy**. 

Instead of moving from place to place, they start to **vibrate more vigorously** around their fixed positions. This corresponds to the first mark in the markscheme, noting the increase in kinetic energy and vibration.

highlights.text: Particles gain kinetic energy and vibrate more vigorously as solid is heated

steps.title: Transition: Melting into liquid
steps.content:
As heating continues, the particles eventually reach the melting point. At this stage, the energy provided is used to **overcome the forces** (such as ionic bonds or intermolecular forces) holding the particles in their fixed lattice. 

The arrangement changes from a fixed, regular structure to a more random one where particles can slide past each other, though they remain close together. This transition is known as **melting**.

highlights.text: Particles overcome forces holding them in fixed positions, causing melting

steps.title: Heating the liquid state
steps.content:
Once the substance is entirely liquid, further heating increases the **kinetic energy** of the particles again. 

In this state, the particles move faster and more randomly, colliding with each other frequently. While they are still close together, their increased energy allows them to move more freely throughout the bulk of the liquid.

highlights.text: Further heating causes liquid particles to gain more kinetic energy

steps.title: Transition: Boiling into gas
steps.content:
Finally, when the liquid reaches its boiling point, the particles gain enough energy to **overcome the intermolecular forces completely**. 

The particles break away from the liquid surface and each other, moving rapidly and independently in all directions. The arrangement becomes highly disordered with large distances between particles, completing the transition into the **gas phase** via boiling or evaporation.

highlights.text: Particles overcome intermolecular forces completely, becoming a gas through evaporation/boiling

steps.title: Analyze the solid state
steps.content:
To start, let's look at the **particle arrangement** in a solid. Particles are held in a regular, fixed lattice by strong attractive forces. 

In terms of **energy analysis**, when we heat the solid, the particles gain average kinetic energy ($E_k$). Because they are in fixed positions, this extra energy causes them to vibrate more vigorously about their fixed points. This aligns with **Topic S1.1.2: The Kinetic Molecular Theory**.

highlights.text: Particles gain kinetic energy and vibrate more vigorously as solid is heated

steps.title: Explain the melting process
steps.content:
As we continue heating, the substance reaches its melting point. Here, a transition in **particle arrangement** occurs:

1. **Arrangement**: The particles gain enough energy to overcome the attractive forces holding them in the fixed lattice.
2. **Energy**: During this phase change, the temperature remains constant. The energy provided is used to increase the **potential energy** ($E_p$) of the particles by breaking the lattice structure, rather than increasing the kinetic energy.

Now, the particles can move past each other while remaining close together.

highlights.text: Particles overcome forces holding them in fixed positions, causing melting

steps.title: Analyze the liquid state
steps.content:
Once the substance is entirely liquid, further heating changes the **energy** profile again. 

The heat energy added now increases the average kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the liquid particles. In terms of **arrangement**, the particles are still in contact but move much faster and more randomly than they did in the solid state.

highlights.text: Further heating causes liquid particles to gain more kinetic energy

steps.title: Describe boiling and gas
steps.content:
Finally, at the boiling point, we see the most dramatic change in **particle arrangement** and **energy**:

- **Arrangement**: Particles overcome intermolecular forces (IMFs) completely. They move very far apart and travel in rapid, random straight-line motion, occupying the entire volume of the container.
- **Energy**: Similar to melting, the temperature stays constant during boiling. The energy added is used to increase the **potential energy** ($E_p$) to move particles far enough apart that IMFs become negligible.

This completes the transition from a structured solid to a disordered gas.

highlights.text: Particles overcome intermolecular forces completely, becoming a gas through evaporation/boiling

steps.title: Heating the solid state
steps.content:
In the first sloped region of a heating curve, heat energy is absorbed by the solid. According to the **Kinetic Molecular Theory (S1.1.2)**, this energy increases the average kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the particles. 

In a solid, particles are held in a fixed lattice. As $E_k$ increases, the particles vibrate more vigorously about their fixed positions, and the temperature of the substance rises.

highlights.text: Particles gain kinetic energy and vibrate more vigorously as solid is heated

steps.title: Melting at the plateau
steps.content:
When the substance reaches its melting point, the heating curve shows a horizontal **plateau**. During this phase change, the temperature remains constant even though heat is still being added.

The energy is now being used to increase the potential energy of the particles by overcoming the attractive forces holding them in their fixed lattice. The particles begin to slide past one another, transitioning from a regular arrangement to a more disordered liquid arrangement.

highlights.text: Particles overcome forces holding them in fixed positions, causing melting

steps.title: Heating the liquid state
steps.content:
After all the solid has melted, the curve enters another sloped region. The added heat energy again increases the average kinetic energy ($E_k$) of the liquid particles.

In this stage, the particles move faster and more randomly, though they remain close together. This increase in $E_k$ is observed as a further rise in temperature.

highlights.text: Further heating causes liquid particles to gain more kinetic energy

steps.title: Boiling to form gas
steps.content:
At the boiling point, the curve reaches a second, longer plateau. Here, the energy is used to completely overcome the **intermolecular forces (S2.2.8)** remaining between the liquid particles.

As these forces are broken, the particles move far apart and occupy the entire volume of the container. The substance transitions into the gaseous state, where particles have negligible attractive forces and move in rapid, random straight-line motion.

highlights.text: Particles overcome intermolecular forces completely, becoming a gas through evaporation/boiling

parts.content: Explain, in terms of kinetic energy and intermolecular forces, the differences in the arrangement and movement of particles in solids, liquids, and gases.
parts.markscheme:
- The kinetic energy of particles is lowest in solids, higher in liquids, and highest in gases [1]
- Intermolecular forces are strongest in solids, weaker in liquids, and weakest or negligible in gases [1]

steps.title: Introduction to the states
steps.content: To explain the differences between solids, liquids, and gases, we look at the balance between **kinetic energy** (which promotes movement) and **intermolecular forces** (which hold particles together). This relates to **Topic S1.1.2: The Kinetic Molecular Theory** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Analyze the solid state
steps.content:
In a solid:
- **Kinetic Energy:** The particles have the lowest kinetic energy, which is not enough to overcome the attractive forces.
- **Intermolecular Forces:** These are at their strongest, locking particles into a fixed, regular lattice arrangement.
- **Movement:** Because of the strong forces, particles cannot move past each other; they only **vibrate** about fixed positions.

highlights.text: Intermolecular forces are strongest in solids
kinetic energy of particles is lowest in solids

steps.title: Analyze the liquid state
steps.content:
In a liquid:
- **Kinetic Energy:** The particles have higher kinetic energy than in a solid.
- **Intermolecular Forces:** These are weaker than in solids but still strong enough to keep particles close together.
- **Movement:** The increased energy allows particles to break out of a fixed lattice. They are arranged randomly but remain in contact, allowing them to **slide or move past one another**, which is why liquids can flow.

steps.title: Analyze the gaseous state
steps.content:
In a gas:
- **Kinetic Energy:** The particles have the highest kinetic energy of the three states.
- **Intermolecular Forces:** These forces are weakest or considered negligible, as the particles are moving too fast for the attractions to hold them together.
- **Movement:** Particles are far apart and arranged randomly. They move **rapidly and randomly** in all directions, filling the volume of their container.

steps.title: Summary of Trends
steps.content:
To secure both marks, ensure you clearly state the relative trends:

1.  **Kinetic Energy (KE):** $\text{Solid} < \text{Liquid} < \text{Gas}$
2.  **Intermolecular Forces (IMF):** $\text{Solid} > \text{Liquid} > \text{Gas}$

steps.title: Identify the Properties
steps.content:
To explain the behavior of matter, we use the **Kinetic Molecular Theory**. We need to compare two specific properties across solids, liquids, and gases:
1. **Kinetic Energy ($E_k$):** The energy of motion.
2. **Intermolecular Forces (IMFs):** The attractive forces between particles.

This is covered in **Topic S1.1: Introduction to the particulate nature of matter**.

steps.title: Compare Kinetic Energy
steps.content:
The kinetic energy of particles is directly related to temperature. Across the three states of matter at a given temperature range, the trend is:

$$\text{Solids (Lowest } E_k) < \text{Liquids (Higher } E_k) < \text{Gases (Highest } E_k)$$

Particles in a gas move much faster and have significantly more energy than those in a solid.

highlights.text: The kinetic energy of particles is lowest in solids, higher in liquids, and highest in gases

steps.title: Compare Intermolecular Forces
steps.content:
Intermolecular forces are the "glue" holding particles together. The strength of these forces depends on how close the particles are and their energy levels. The trend is the opposite of kinetic energy:

$$\text{Solids (Strongest)} > \text{Liquids (Weaker)} > \text{Gases (Negligible)}$$

In gases, the particles are moving so fast and are so far apart that IMFs have almost no effect.

highlights.text: Intermolecular forces are strongest in solids, weaker in liquids, and weakest or negligible in gases

steps.title: Impact on Solids
steps.content: In **solids**, the intermolecular forces are so strong (and $E_k$ is so low) that particles are locked into a **fixed, regular lattice arrangement**. Because they lack the energy to overcome these forces, their movement is limited to **vibration around fixed positions**.

steps.title: Impact on Liquids
steps.content: In **liquids**, the higher kinetic energy allows particles to partially overcome the IMFs. This results in a **random/irregular arrangement** where particles remain close together but are no longer fixed. Consequently, they can **slide or flow over one another**.

steps.title: Impact on Gases
steps.content: In **gases**, the kinetic energy is high enough to completely overcome intermolecular forces. The particles are **widely spaced with no regular arrangement** and move with **rapid, random translation motion**, filling the entire volume of their container.
